1: <header>Swine manure treatment technologies as drivers for circular economy in agribusiness: A techno-economic and life cycle assessment approach </header>
C.E. Hollas H.C. Rodrigues b , A.C. Bolsan , B. Venturin a , M. Bortoli° F.G. Antes d , R.L.R. Steinmetz d , A. Kunz a,d,*

<list>a Universidade Estadual do Oeste do Paraná, UNIOESTE/CCET/PGEAGRI, Cascavel, PR, Brazil 
b Universidade Tecnológica Federal do Paraná, 85660-000 Dois Vizinhos, PR, Brazil 
c Universidade Tecnológica Federal do Paraná, 85601-970 Francisco Beltrão, PR, Brazil 
d Embrapa Suínos e Aves, 89715-899 Concórdia, SC, Brazil</list>
2:
<list>The management of swine manure from the perspective of circular economy is studied. 
Anaerobic digestion is the key to circular economy of the treatment systems. 
Life cycle analysis results indicated miti- gations of up to 90 % in CO2 emissions. 
Effluents treatment results energy, fertil- izers, and water reuse, without residues. 
Anaerobic digestion with digestate treat- ment is the most viable configuration.</list>
4: Keywords: Anaerobic digestion Energy recovery Livestock Nutrient recovery Water reuse Waste management
5: Anaerobic digestion has been employed as a technology capable of adding value to waste coupled with environmental impact mitigation. However, many issues need to be elucidated to ensure the systems viability based on this technol- ogy. In this sense, the present study evaluated technically, environmentally, and economically, four configurations of swine waste treatment systems focused on the promotion of decarbonization and circularity of the swine chain. For this, a reference plant, based on a compact treatment process named SISTRATES (Portuguese acronym for swine ef- fluent treatment system) was adopted to serve as a model for comparison and validation. The results showed the im- portance of prioritization of the energy recuperation routes through anaerobic digestion, providing increased economic benefits and minimizing environmental damage. Thus, the SISTRATES configuration was the one that pre- sented the best designs in a circular context, maximizing the recovery of energy and nutrients, along with the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions, ensuring the sustainability of the pig production chain.

7: The need to overcome climate impacts without compromising the fu- ture development of nations has intensified the search for alternative solu- tions to resource management (Milhorance et al., 2022; van den Oever et al., 2021). The energy matrix based on fossil sources is no longer an option to supply the needs of current and future society, the depletion of natural resources and the damage to the environment promoted by the un- bridled use of non-renewable sources have severely compromised the envi- ronment (Li et al., 2018). This is evidenced by the latest report (AR6) of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), which pointed out that greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions must be drastically reduced by 2050 to halt global warming (GW) (IPCC, 2021).

In this sense, the productive sectors have increasingly invested in technologies to promote the decarbonization of the activity, mitigating en- vironmental damage without harming the development of the chain (Duan et al., 2020). Among these productive sectors, agriculture, and especially livestock, stands out as a major emitter of GHGs, about two-thirds of all emissions from agriculture come from livestock activities (FAO, 2020). In addition, the waste generated in this production chain has impacts on other sectors of the environment, according to FAO (2020) in the year 2018 the sector was responsible for the emission of 60 million tons of N, about 35 % of which was lost in gas form (e.g. N2, N20, NOx, NH3), and another 58 % leached into ground water and superficial water, thus the management of this waste is fundamental to compromise future sustainable development (Li et al., 2021).

Thus, a circular-based economy aimed at resource utilization, especially with the insertion of waste in the value chain, can help to reduce environ- mental pressure and mitigate dependence on non-renewable sources (Awasthi et al., 2022; Ugwu et al., 2022). These residues present a high potential for the generation of products with added value, especially the recovery of energy, nutrients, and fuels, among other products that can help change the thinking, reinserting the waste with renewable sources of resources (Fermoso et al., 2018; Kapoor et al., 2020). Therefore, anaerobic digestion (AD) has been widely explored as a technology capable of pro- moting the management of livestock waste combined with the recovery of renewable energy present in the organic fraction of these materials (Tian et al., 2021; Yong et al., 2021). Several studies have been conducted in recent years exploring various characteristics of the technology, such as studies on pretreatment (Amaral et al., 2016); microorganisms activity (Rico et al., 2017); co-digestion (Fernando-Foncillas and Varrone, 2021); temperature effects (Schmidt et al., 2019); techno-economic characteristics al., 2019; Y. Li et al., 2020); resource recovery (Cândido et al., 2022; Fermoso et al., 2018; Kvist and Aryal, 2019); among many other aspects related to the anaerobic digestion process and fundamental for the contin- uous improvement of the technology.

To supply this demand for systems that address various aspects, combin- ing treatment and resource recovery, SISTRATES (Portuguese acronym for swine effluent treatment system) was developed (Kunz et al., 2021). A module-based system for the solid and liquid fractions of swine wastewater, combining anaerobic digestion with biological nitrogen removal and chem- ical phosphorus recovery, generating clean energy, biofertilizers and reuse water, maximizing the environmental benefits of the treatment system in a circular concept (Cândido et al., 2022). However, these research and tech- nological routes present certain implementation barriers, an integrated solution that allows maximizing the use of waste while reducing costs is still a challenge (Gigli et al., 2019; J. Zhang et al., 2021).

Faced with SO many treatment configurations, issues such as technical and economic feasibility and the understanding of the process life cycle must be elucidated, since the treatment configuration adopted can promote different impacts, both in technical, economic and especially environmental terms (Awasthi et al., 2021). This has been verified in several studies on the life cycle and economics of treatment systems focusing on AD, such as those developed by Aui et al. (2019), that studied the feasibility an AD plant for confined livestock and found that scenario emissions range from 82 gCO kWh with credits ranging from $0.002 to $0.017 per kWh;

already Finzi et al. (2020) evaluated the feasibility of a collective treatment system for energy recovery and nutrient removal from livestock manure and found a profit of €1.61 ton treated manure and an average global warming potential reduction of 70 % (resulting in 20.79 kg CO2 eq Corbala-Robles et al. (2018) compared the effects of treating swine manure through AD with direct application to the field of untreated manure (untreated scenario), and found a 46 % reduction in the impact of climate change, resulting in a 9.80 kgCO2 eq while Duan et al. (2020) evaluated the life cycle of different treatment technologies for swine manure, observed that the impacts for GW potential ranged from 11 to 64.7 kg CO2 eq whereas Li et al. (2018) evaluated the life cycle of AD for treating agricultural organic waste and found that the GW potential ranged from -183 to 2900 kg CO2 eq

Tian et al. (2021) concluded that treatment processes cannot be consid- ered sustainable if emissions from the processes cannot be controlled or minimized, thus, the decision of the best technology is a complex and holis- tic process, which should be based on several factors considering environ- mental, economic, technical, and social aspects. Although SISTRATES is a consolidated technology, no study on its environmental viability, and es- pecially no comparison with other possible technological routes has been done, which compromises the robustness of this treatment system. In this sense, the present study aimed to compare different swine manure treat- ment arrangements, derived from SISTRATES technology, verifying the feasibility of these treatment routes, through the alteration of the reaction units for AD, comparing the use of reactors type CSTR (Continuous Stirred-tank Reactor), UASB (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket), and CLB (covered lagoon biodigesters). In addition to evaluating the effect of the change in the system solids flux, the technologies of biological nitrogen removal and chemical phosphorus recovery, with a bias towards water reuse. All this, using a techno-economic and life cycle approach to check the impacts that the adopted changes caused in these aspects, to determine the most sustainable condition for the treatment of swine manure in a circu- lar economy context.

To supply this demand for systems that address various aspects, combin- ing treatment and resource recovery, SISTRATES (Portuguese acronym for swine effluent treatment system) was developed (Kunz et al., 2021). A module-based system for the solid and liquid fractions of swine wastewater, combining anaerobic digestion with biological nitrogen removal and chem- ical phosphorus recovery, generating clean energy, biofertilizers and reuse water, maximizing the environmental benefits of the treatment system in a circular concept (Cândido et al., 2022). However, these research and tech- nological routes present certain implementation barriers, an integrated solution that allows maximizing the use of waste while reducing costs is still a challenge (Gigli et al., 2019; J. Zhang et al., 2021).

Faced with SO many treatment configurations, issues such as technical and economic feasibility and the understanding of the process life cycle must be elucidated, since the treatment configuration adopted can promote different impacts, both in technical, economic and especially environmental terms (Awasthi et al., 2021). This has been verified in several studies on the life cycle and economics of treatment systems focusing on AD, such as those developed by Aui et al. (2019), that studied the feasibility an AD plant for confined livestock and found that scenario emissions range from 82 gCO kWh with credits ranging from $0.002 to $0.017 per kWh;

already Finzi et al. (2020) evaluated the feasibility of a collective treatment system for energy recovery and nutrient removal from livestock manure and found a profit of €1.61 ton treated manure and an average global warming potential reduction of 70 % (resulting in 20.79 kg CO2 eq Corbala-Robles et al. (2018) compared the effects of treating swine manure through AD with direct application to the field of untreated manure (untreated scenario), and found a 46 % reduction in the impact of climate change, resulting in a 9.80 kgCO2 eq while Duan et al. (2020) evaluated the life cycle of different treatment technologies for swine manure, observed that the impacts for GW potential ranged from 11 to 64.7 kg CO2 eq whereas Li et al. (2018) evaluated the life cycle of AD for treating agricultural organic waste and found that the GW potential ranged from -183 to 2900 kg CO2 eq

Tian et al. (2021) concluded that treatment processes cannot be consid- ered sustainable if emissions from the processes cannot be controlled or minimized, thus, the decision of the best technology is a complex and holis- tic process, which should be based on several factors considering environ- mental, economic, technical, and social aspects. Although SISTRATES is a consolidated technology, no study on its environmental viability, and es- pecially no comparison with other possible technological routes has been done, which compromises the robustness of this treatment system. In this sense, the present study aimed to compare different swine manure treat- ment arrangements, derived from SISTRATES technology, verifying the feasibility of these treatment routes, through the alteration of the reaction units for AD, comparing the use of reactors type CSTR (Continuous Stirred-tank Reactor), UASB (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket), and CLB (covered lagoon biodigesters). In addition to evaluating the effect of the change in the system solids flux, the technologies of biological nitrogen removal and chemical phosphorus recovery, with a bias towards water reuse. All this, using a techno-economic and life cycle approach to check the impacts that the adopted changes caused in these aspects, to determine the most sustainable condition for the treatment of swine manure in a circu- lar economy context.
12: The need to overcome climate impacts without compromising the fu- ture development of nations has intensified the search for alternative solu- tions to resource management (Milhorance et al., 2022; van den Oever et al., 2021). The energy matrix based on fossil sources is no longer an option to supply the needs of current and future society, the depletion of natural resources and the damage to the environment promoted by the un- bridled use of non-renewable sources have severely compromised the envi- ronment (Li et al., 2018). This is evidenced by the latest report (AR6) of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), which pointed out that greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions must be drastically reduced by 2050 to halt global warming (GW) (IPCC, 2021).

In this sense, the productive sectors have increasingly invested in technologies to promote the decarbonization of the activity, mitigating en- vironmental damage without harming the development of the chain (Duan et al., 2020). Among these productive sectors, agriculture, and especially livestock, stands out as a major emitter of GHGs, about two-thirds of all emissions from agriculture come from livestock activities (FAO, 2020). In addition, the waste generated in this production chain has impacts on other sectors of the environment, according to FAO (2020) in the year 2018 the sector was responsible for the emission of 60 million tons of N, about 35 % of which was lost in gas form (e.g. N2, N20, NOx, NH3), and another 58 % leached into ground water and superficial water, thus the management of this waste is fundamental to compromise future sustainable development (Li et al., 2021).

Thus, a circular-based economy aimed at resource utilization, especially with the insertion of waste in the value chain, can help to reduce environ- mental pressure and mitigate dependence on non-renewable sources (Awasthi et al., 2022; Ugwu et al., 2022). These residues present a high potential for the generation of products with added value, especially the recovery of energy, nutrients, and fuels, among other products that can help change the thinking, reinserting the waste with renewable sources of resources (Fermoso et al., 2018; Kapoor et al., 2020). Therefore, anaerobic digestion (AD) has been widely explored as a technology capable of pro- moting the management of livestock waste combined with the recovery of renewable energy present in the organic fraction of these materials (Tian et al., 2021; Yong et al., 2021). Several studies have been conducted in recent years exploring various characteristics of the technology, such as studies on pretreatment (Amaral et al., 2016); microorganisms activity (Rico et al., 2017); co-digestion (Fernando-Foncillas and Varrone, 2021); temperature effects (Schmidt et al., 2019); techno-economic characteristics al., 2019; Y. Li et al., 2020); resource recovery (Cândido et al., 2022; Fermoso et al., 2018; Kvist and Aryal, 2019); among many other aspects related to the anaerobic digestion process and fundamental for the contin- uous improvement of the technology.

A module-based system for the solid and liquid fractions of swine wastewater, combining anaerobic digestion with biological nitrogen removal and chem- ical phosphorus recovery, generating clean energy, biofertilizers and reuse water, maximizing the environmental benefits of the treatment system in a circular concept (Cândido et al., 2022). However, these research and tech- nological routes present certain implementation barriers, an integrated solution that allows maximizing the use of waste while reducing costs is still a challenge (Gigli et al., 2019; J. Zhang et al., 2021).

Faced with SO many treatment configurations, issues such as technical and economic feasibility and the understanding of the process life cycle must be elucidated, since the treatment configuration adopted can promote different impacts, both in technical, economic and especially environmental terms (Awasthi et al., 2021). This has been verified in several studies on the life cycle and economics of treatment systems focusing on AD, such as those developed by Aui et al. (2019), that studied the feasibility an AD plant for confined livestock and found that scenario emissions range from 82 gCO kWh with credits ranging from $0.002 to $0.017 per kWh;

already Finzi et al. (2020) evaluated the feasibility of a collective treatment system for energy recovery and nutrient removal from livestock manure and found a profit of €1.61 ton treated manure and an average global warming potential reduction of 70 % (resulting in 20.79 kg CO2 eq Corbala-Robles et al. (2018) compared the effects of treating swine manure through AD with direct application to the field of untreated manure (untreated scenario), and found a 46 % reduction in the impact of climate change, resulting in a 9.80 kgCO2 eq while Duan et al. (2020) evaluated the life cycle of different treatment technologies for swine manure, observed that the impacts for GW potential ranged from 11 to 64.7 kg CO2 eq whereas Li et al. (2018) evaluated the life cycle of AD for treating agricultural organic waste and found that the GW potential ranged from -183 to 2900 kg CO2 eq

Tian et al. 14: In this study four scenarios for swine manure treatment were evaluated (Fig. 1). The first scenario under study (S1) corresponds to the reference scenario and comprises the SISTRATES® (a Portuguese acronym for swine effluent treatment system) (Fig. 1a). The system designed to be used in three additive modules: biogas generation and reduction of organic load (Bio module), nitrogen removal (N module), and removal and recov- ery of phosphorus (P module). This system, designed for a flow rate of 250 m³ is in full-scale operation, installed in a farm located in Videira, Santa Catarina - Brazil (27°02'38.8"S 51°05'35.7"W) and is presented in more detail in Cândido et al. (2022). From this scenario (SISTRATESR) three variations were simulated, following the same modular concept for the treatment and utilization of swine effluents.

The second scenario (S2) (Fig. 1b), focused on the removal of nitrogen and the adequate supply of carbon to the denitrification process. Thus, the Bio module counted only on the digestion of sludge and solid waste in the CSTR, with the digestate use as biofertilizer and the biogas energetic utilization in a heat and power cogeneration unit. The effluent was di- rected, after the solid-liquid separation (SLS) (sieving followed by sedimen- tation), directly to N module. This module is composed of a nitrification and denitrification system (Hollas et al., 2019), similar to the modified Ludzack- Ettinger process (MLE) used in the SISTRATES®. After N module, the efflu- ent was submitted to the process of phosphorus chemical precipitation using calcium hydroxide, as performed in the reference system, resulting in calcium phosphate and the final effluent discharged into the water body.

The third scenario (S3) (Fig. 1c), the CLB was replaced by four UASB reactors (Lins et al., 2020). In addition, the system flow started with SLS through sieving followed by settling. The solid waste is diverted to the CSTR reactor and the digestate combined with N module sludge applied
CSTR|||discharge River Sieve|Settling tank|module P module|discharge River CHP Storage|Fertilization|Ca3(PO)2|b) S2

Fig. 1. Schematic representation of the scenarios under study. a) Scenario 1 (S1 - SISTRATES), b) Scenario 2 (S2), c) Scenario 3 (S3), d) Scenario 4 (S4). (CHP: heat and power cogeneration unit; Ca3(PO4)2: calcium phosphate).

in the soil. The biogas generated in the process is utilized in a cogeneration unit for heat and power. The effluent from the UASBs was directed to N module, as in the reference scenario (S1). While for P module, the removal of phosphorus was performed through the addition of sodium hydroxide, to promote the precipitation of K-struvite, having the final effluent discharged into the water body.
The scenario 4 (S4) (Fig. 1d) focused on the generation of a fertilizer at the end of the process, which can be exported outside the production area,

through the composting of sludge from the system. In this scenario, the pro- cess starts with SLS by sieving followed by sedimentation, with the solids di- rected to the CSTR and the liquid fraction to the N module (as S1). P module was of the same as used in S3, and the final effluent was reused in the pro- duction facilities for cleaning purposes. The biogas generated was converted into electricity in a cogeneration unit, as in the other scenarios.
A technical description of the systems in terms of design and operating conditions is presented in more details in the supplementary material (SM).

The feasibility was evaluated based on their compatibility with the char- acteristics of the effluent and operating conditions applied in the treatment processes. The efficiency of the interconnected routes was considered, i.e., how the configurations under study would affect the performance of the entire treatment process (Chong et al., 2012). The operating conditions in terms of efficiency for each stage (described in Tables 1.2a, 1.4a, 1.4a, 1.5a of the SM) were stipulated based on process literature with similar operating conditions, from the plant monitoring data (Cândido et al., 2022) and when absent by means of bench experiments, thus allowing the inputs and outputs to be stipulated based on the modules mass balances (Table 1.1a).

18: In this study four scenarios for swine manure treatment were evaluated (Fig. 1). The first scenario under study (S1) corresponds to the reference scenario and comprises the SISTRATES® (a Portuguese acronym for swine effluent treatment system) (Fig. 1a). The system designed to be used in three additive modules: biogas generation and reduction of organic load (Bio module), nitrogen removal (N module), and removal and recov- ery of phosphorus (P module). This system, designed for a flow rate of 250 m³ is in full-scale operation, installed in a farm located in Videira, Santa Catarina - Brazil (27°02'38.8"S 51°05'35.7"W) and is presented in more detail in Cândido et al. (2022). From this scenario (SISTRATESR) three variations were simulated, following the same modular concept for the treatment and utilization of swine effluents.

The second scenario (S2) (Fig. 1b), focused on the removal of nitrogen and the adequate supply of carbon to the denitrification process. Thus, the Bio module counted only on the digestion of sludge and solid waste in the CSTR, with the digestate use as biofertilizer and the biogas energetic utilization in a heat and power cogeneration unit. The effluent was di- rected, after the solid-liquid separation (SLS) (sieving followed by sedimen- tation), directly to N module. This module is composed of a nitrification and denitrification system (Hollas et al., 2019), similar to the modified Ludzack- Ettinger process (MLE) used in the SISTRATES®. After N module, the efflu- ent was submitted to the process of phosphorus chemical precipitation using calcium hydroxide, as performed in the reference system, resulting in calcium phosphate and the final effluent discharged into the water body.

The third scenario (S3) (Fig. 1c), the CLB was replaced by four UASB reactors (Lins et al., 2020). In addition, the system flow started with SLS through sieving followed by settling. The solid waste is diverted to the CSTR reactor and the digestate combined with N module sludge applied
CSTR|||discharge River Sieve|Settling tank|module P module|discharge River CHP Storage|Fertilization|Ca3(PO)2|b) S2

Fig. 1. Schematic representation of the scenarios under study. a) Scenario 1 (S1 - SISTRATES), b) Scenario 2 (S2), c) Scenario 3 (S3), d) Scenario 4 (S4). (CHP: heat and power cogeneration unit; Ca3(PO4)2: calcium phosphate).

in the soil. The biogas generated in the process is utilized in a cogeneration unit for heat and power. The effluent from the UASBs was directed to N module, as in the reference scenario (S1). While for P module, the removal of phosphorus was performed through the addition of sodium hydroxide, to promote the precipitation of K-struvite, having the final effluent discharged into the water body.
The scenario 4 (S4) (Fig. 1d) focused on the generation of a fertilizer at the end of the process, which can be exported outside the production area,

through the composting of sludge from the system. In this scenario, the pro- cess starts with SLS by sieving followed by sedimentation, with the solids di- rected to the CSTR and the liquid fraction to the N module (as S1). P module was of the same as used in S3, and the final effluent was reused in the pro- duction facilities for cleaning purposes. The biogas generated was converted into electricity in a cogeneration unit, as in the other scenarios.
A technical description of the systems in terms of design and operating conditions is presented in more details in the supplementary material (SM).

The feasibility was evaluated based on their compatibility with the char- acteristics of the effluent and operating conditions applied in the treatment processes. The efficiency of the interconnected routes was considered, i.e., how the configurations under study would affect the performance of the entire treatment process (Chong et al., 2012). The operating conditions in terms of efficiency for each stage (described in Tables 1.2a, 1.4a, 1.4a, 1.5a of the SM) were stipulated based on process literature with similar operating conditions, from the plant monitoring data (Cândido et al., 2022) and when absent by means of bench experiments, thus allowing the inputs and outputs to be stipulated based on the modules mass balances (Table 1.1a).
19: Life cycle analysis (LCA) was used to evaluate the environmental as- pects of the scenarios under study, making it possible to verify the possible environmental impacts and/or benefits caused by the different arrange- ments of the treatment systems. The evaluation was done by the Software SimaPro 9.2.0.1 (PRé, 2022) according to the International Organization for Standardization (ISO 14040, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006).

For the LCA an attributional modeling with average data allied to the substitution approach was adopted, in which the by-products generated from the treatment (electricity, heat, and fertilizers) were used as substi- tutes for commercial products, thus avoiding their production and environ- mental burden. The system boundaries included all the steps present in the scenarios described in Section 2.1 and presented in Fig. 1; i.e., they encom- passed SLS, AD, biological nitrogen removal and phosphorus chemical precipitation, water reuse, as well as storage and/or composting of the sludge, followed by application as fertilizer. In addition to the use of biogas for co-generation of electricity and heat, in the combined heat and power (CHP) plant, and the replacement of the generated energy and biofertilizers by their commercial equivalents. As the systems present more than one product, i.e., several outputs, the functional unit (FU) was defined as being the treatment of 1 m³ of raw manure, to equate the analysis of the scenarios (Corbala-Robles et al., 2018).

For all scenarios, the impacts related to the construction and decommissioning stages of the physical facilities were not included in the evaluation, since many studies have found that the impacts of these stages (when considering the life of the enterprise) are low compared to the ongo- ing impacts of the operation stage, as presented by Esteves et al. (2019). Thus, the objective was to verify the impacts associated with changes in waste management in operational terms.

In addition to the scenarios under study (described in Section 2.1) a baseline scenario (SB) was adopted (described in the SM, Fig. 2.6a), consid- ering the swine waste usual management in Brazil, which consists of storage (deep pit) for about 80 days and subsequent soil application (IMA, 2021).

To build the systems inventory (Table 1, Table 2, Table 3) all inputs and outputs, emissions and energy demands of each module were considered, according to the flows presented in Fig. 1. The data for both scenarios were based on measurements and monitoring performed in the plant (S1) and applied to the others whenever possible, considering similar operating conditions; or estimated based on the Ecoinvet 3.8 database (Ecoinvent, 2021), and/or pertinent literature. More detailed information about the construction of the inventory is described in the SM.

For the impact assessment of the systems under study, the ReCiPe Midpoint (H) 2016 evaluation methodology (Huijbregts et al., 2017) was used, as it is a method widely used in treatment system evaluations to deter- mine the environmental impact, problem-oriented and global in scope (Briones-Hidrovo et al., 2020). Considering the most prominent environ- mental issues in the context of Brazilian livestock, the impact categories were evaluated: global warming (GW); stratospheric ozone depletion (SOD); ozone formation - terrestrial ecosystem (OFTE); terrestrial acidifica- tion (TA); freshwater eutrophication (FE) and terrestrial ecotoxicity (TE). In addition, the interpretation of the LCA results was performed following the "Guide to Interpreting Life Cycle Assessment Results" by Zampori et al. (2016), which defines the significant aspects to be considered when inter- preting the LCA results.

A sensitivity analysis was performed varying the percentages of emis- sions from the fertilization stage by 40 % and 60 %, since this stage had a significant contribution to the impacts of the scenarios and can be mitigated depending on the practices adopted in the fertilization process. According to Finzi et al. (2019), incorporation can promote a mitigation of up to 60 % in gas emissions compared to spraying effluents in the field, which can impact the environmental designs of the scenarios studied.

34: Once the process flows and equipment sizes were specified (as pre- sented in section 3.A of the SM), the economic parameters such as capital expenditures (CAPEX) and operating expenditures (OPEX), net present value (NPV) and internal rate of return (IRR) were estimated, as presented in 4.A Economic Aspects of the supplemental material. These values define the financial viability of a project and determine the economic benefits for implementing the technology, and are used to compare the scenarios under study (Gigli et al., 2019).
35: The technical performance of the different scenarios under study high- lighted how changes in treatment system configurations affected the

individual processes in a cascading effect. In addition, all scenarios showed applicability, with minor issues that favored or worsened the results of each process. In the first stage of the systems, i.e., SLS by screening, similar values in terms of separation efficiency were adopted in all scenarios based on the values of the reference scenario (S1), 12 % efficiency for total solids (TS) removal, % for phosphorus (P) removal, 2 % for total ni- trogen (TN) removal and 8 % for total organic carbon (TOC) removal (Table 1.3a of the SM) (Fig. 2). These values are consistent with average values in the literature, such as those presented by Amaral et al. (2016), who studied the effect of SLS on biogas generation capacity for swine ma- nure, and screening efficiency that ranged from 6 % to 21 %.

However, a preliminary study (Hollas et al., 2021) found average solids removal efficiencies around 30 % for screening, emphasizing the effect of manure management, especially in terms of storage time, within the facili- ties as a critical factor in the process of solids removal via mechanical de- vices. The management inside the animal production facilities also affects the process efficiency, due to this, the volumes of sludge generated were different in the scenarios, being the ratio of 7.40E-03 Msludge Mmanure for S1 (Table 1.7a of the SM), while for S2, S3 and S4 considering an ideal man- agement, as proposed by Hollas et al. (2021), the volumetric ratio was 3.50E-02 Msludge Mmanure In addition to compromising the SLS process, the management of effluents in production facilities promotes the loss of GHG, especially methane, due to the degradation process that begins in the storage pits, reducing the potential of this waste and contributing to the GHG emissions (Im et al., 2020).

The second stage of SLS via sedimentation, the considered removal effi- ciencies (Table 1.3a of the SM) were distinct once it was identified that the management of the real scale system (S1) affected the process efficiency. Thus, for this scenario the removal efficiencies are 33 % for TS, % for total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN), 43 % for P, 7 % for the removal of TOC, besides sludge the generation compared to the initial volume about 10 While for the other scenarios (S2, S3 and S4), removal values were adopted of 60 % for TS, 2% for TAN, 80 for P, 30% for TOC. These initial stages are fundamental for functioning good operation of the subsequent units, a loss in efficiency of these processes is indicative of problems with effluent management, either within the production units, sludge manage- ment of the separation units, or in terms of overload, such as flows rates that exceed the design ones compromising the entire treatment (Vanotti et al., 2018).

In relation the Bio module, the divergences in the configurations pre- sented impacts on the systems not only in terms of biogas production, but also in its composition. The S1 presents satisfactory values in terms of bio- gas production, with 7.85 Mmanure, 76 % coming from the CLBs (0.56 Nmbiogas kgsv1), that is, the liquid fraction, and 24 % from the CSTR (0.38 Nmbiogas kgsv) that is, the solid fraction. This production results in an electrical energy generation of 1.24 kWh Nmbiogas, with an estimated heat cogeneration of 84.76 MJ Nmbiogas (Cândido et al., 2022).

The S2 have only the biogas generated from the solids system, which result in a production of 5.92 mmanure kgsv1), the value is higher than the reference scenario due to the higher efficiency of the sedimentation process that generates a sludge with a higher content of VS, in addition, the heat cogeneration is estimated at 63.97 MJ Nmbiogas-

For the S3 the liquid fraction is directed to four UASBs, reactors which leads to the production of a more energetically rich biogas, that is, with higher methane contents (around 75 %) (Zhao et al., 2021). Rico et al. (2017) highlighted that the use of UASB type reactors for the treatment of swine waste is an attractive alternative, according to the authors the combi- nation of lower hydraulic retention time (HRT), with a high organic loading rate (OLR), provides a better methanogenic activity that reflects in the rate of volumetric production of methane, resulting in a biogas with up to 80 of CH4, that is a very important point for energy aspect of utilization. Added to this, the better efficiency of the SLS, results in a biogas production of 7.34 Nmbiogas Mmanure, being, 32 % of the UASB (0.70 and 68 % of the CSTR (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsv1), and the electrical generation considered of 1.38 kWh Nmbiogas with 79.32 MJ Nmbiogas of cogenerated heat.

For S1 and S3: P1-Raw effluent; P2-Sieve effluent; P3-Settling tank effluent; P4-CLB/UASB effluent; P5-N Module effluent; P6-P Module effluent. (Were C/C = Characteristics of raw manure and effluent at the end of each module. C = TSo: 16380 mg L-1; VS0: 11440 mg L-1; NH3-No: 1293.6 mg L-1 P0: 462.64 mg L-1; K0: 757.35 mg L-1; TOC: 3585.37 mg L-1.

In S4 only the sludge is used energetically by means of AD, but in this scenario the sludge resulting from N module is not directed to the CSTR re- actor reducing by half the input flow rate, but practically doubling the VS concentration, with this the biogas production was estimated at 5.30 Mmanure (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsu1 already the heat cogeneration was 57.28 MJ Nmbiogas

The improvement in the flow of solids directly affects the strategy of en- ergy use, since the scenarios with the absence of the reactor for the use of the liquid fraction (S2 and S4), do not have such significant losses com- pared to the other scenarios due to the better existing management. Favor- ing the digestion of solids through the CSTR reactor is important to the system not only to ensure the life of the CLB type reactors (present in S1) or UASB type (present in S3), but also because of the reduction of the oscil- lations of gas production due to the year seasonality effect, the cold months tend to reduce the gas production in reactors that do not have a heating system (Yan et al., 2022). Schmidt et al. (2019) found that the biogas pro- duction was affected by 30 % with the reduction of 7 °C in the system, compromising the stability of the process and the methane production. Thus, promoting efficient separation and the use of sludge reactors ensures that the solids will have a degradation with less interference all year round, maximized the biogas productivity of the plant, reducing the need for more reactor units.

After the AD, the effluent is directed to the N module. All scenarios pres- ent operational potentials with the conditions imposed at the module inlet. For this process one of the main factors that impact the different scenarios is the bioavailability of organic carbon at the system influent (Hollas et al., 2019). Since in the absence or insufficient supply of organic matter, the de- nitrification process occurs incompletely and can cause damage in terms of reducing the buffering potential, reducing the system pH and compromising the removal efficiency (Lee et al., 2019; Shi et al., 2020). Besides being potentially dangerous to the environment due to the higher probability of nitrous oxide (N20) losses from incomplete denitrification, since N2O is one of the intermediates of the process (Bortoli et al., 2022; Dai et al., 2021).

In the scenarios where the previous step was AD (S1 and S3), the carbon input is limited, since it was previously consumed by the AD process, thus in hot months where the efficiency of the Bio module is above average, there is a risk of the N removal system collapsing if organic matter supplementa- tion is not done (Wang et al., 2018). With the reduction in denitrification and the consequent decrease in pH, the presence of nitrite must be carefully monitored SO that inhibition by nitrous acid does not affect the system (Jiang et al., 2019). However this can be easily solved by diverting a frac- tion of the Bio module's input flow, ensuring the operation of the N module, as demonstrated by Awad et al. (2022), who found that increasing the carbon supply in the anoxic zone by means of a by-pass improved TN removal efficiency by about %.

For S2 and S4, the high availability of carbon guarantees the efficient functioning of N module, however the increase of dissolved organic carbon and the fraction of solids in the effluent that enters the system causes another problem, related to sludge generation (Table 1.5a of the SM). The higher carbon bioavailability favors the growth of heterotrophic organisms, which translates into a high generation of sludge, besides increasing the oxygen demand of the system, either through the difficulty in mass transfer, due to the higher content of solids, or by the demand for oxidation of these organic compounds (Steuernagel et al., 2018). Consequently the energy ex- penditure of the system is high (Table 1), being considered about 2 times higher than the reference scenario. Wang et al. (2021) reported that nitrifi- cation was severely compromised by the excess of organic compounds, also affecting the flocculation and sedimentation capacity of the nitrifying bio- mass, which adds to the generated sludge management problem (W. M. et al., 2020).

The high generation of sludge in the N module can be considered a problem beyond the energy aspect, since this waste does not present high fertilizing power (Fig. 3), nor even high potential for biogas generation (75 + 8 mLNCH4 gvs ). In S2, where the surplus sludge from N module is directed to the CSTR, this sludge ends up diluting the solids concentration up to 49 %, without having large contributions in biogas generation,

increasing the heating system demand about 1.7 times compared to the other scenarios. The high volume of sludge presents a high cost of treat- ment, according to Domini et al. (2022) for the year 2021, the average price of sludge treatment in Northern Italy averaged 176.9 ton consid- ering loading, transport, recovery/disposal and analysis costs. Not promot- ing the generation of excess sludge in the systems should be a priority since it has techno-economic consequences in the treatment plants. Thus, the elimination of the AD systems predecessors to this module can bring losses to the process operation.

P module the effluent characteristics resulted from N module will directly reflect on the performance of the P precipitation process (Table 1.6a of the SM). In S1 the Ca(OH)2 demand is about 4.5 times lower than for S2, because the higher alkalinity present in the effluent resulting from N module in S2, has a higher buffer capacity requiring more Ca(OH)2 for pH adjustment to the ideal precipitation conditions. Also the more and impure sludge is generated due to the remained TOC concentration that is coprecipitated. Christensen et al. (2022) studied the effects of organic matter on P precipita- tion and found a relatively large difference in the amount of precipitate (>50% for the liquid sample with a low carbon content compared to the other samples with a higher organic matter content, which also reflected in a precipitate with 20 % organic materials in the composition.

In S3 and S4 the precipitation of K-struvite was simulated to promote the removal of K, since this nutrient has no effective removal in any of the treatment modules. Theoretically equimolar ratios of K:P:Mg would be sufficient to promote K-struvite precipitation, so as this assumption was met in all scenarios, this process was simulated with the addition of NaOH in the effluent up to pH 9 (Le et al., 2021). With this, the NaOH de- mand would be 0.17 kg for S3 and 0.50 for S4, according to the test performed (described in 5.A in the SM). This higher demand in S4 is due to the same reason as S2, due to the higher alkalinity of the efflu- ent and consequent buffering of the medium. Although theoretically the ratios of K:P:Mg meet the premises of the process, the presence of Ca and residual NH3-N in the effluent, possibly would not promote the effective precipitation of K-struvite, but the precipitation of calcium phosphate or struvite, not removing K as expected. Furthermore, the ratio of K is about 10 times higher than the ratios of Mg and P, thus, supplementation of P and Mg in the effluent would be necessary to promote K removal.

Kabdasli et al. (2022) discussed that the process of K-struvite precipita- tion at stoichiometric doses can only be used for partial recovery of K as simulated in the present study, the authors verified the removal of about 40% of potassium, at equimolar ratios at pH values of 8 to 10, the improve- ment in removal efficiency was obtained when the system was overdosed with 100 % Mg and P and a pH of 10 resulting in 85 % recovery of K. With this, the simulated pH of 9 would possibly not be enough to favor ef- ficient K-struvite precipitation, which would demand more reagents for the process raising treatment costs. Thus, this alternative of P and K recovery, by the addition of NaOH was not configured as an effective way to jointly remove K and P from the effluent in the process under study.

S4 presents an additional step, related to the management of final sludge through composting. Although these sludges are generally stabi- lized, once they were submitted to the AD process, composting would act as a technology to reduce the humidity of these effluents, facilitating the ex- port/storage of this material (Sharara and Sadaka, 2018). At this stage, if the premises of the composting process are met, the process would occur ef- ficiently, which would result in the generation of a compost with 50 less volume and with a concentration of 0.13% of total nitrogen (TN), 0.12% of TOC, 0.12 9 of K and 0.14% of P. These values can still be im- proved, Z. Zhang et al. (2021) performed a meta-analysis and verified that the adjustment of the initial properties of the substrate, combined with control of the conditions of the composting process and even the appli- cation of additives can promote an improvement in the process and contrib- ute to the generation of a compost with better fertilizing quality. With this, there is the possibility of exploring this process as an alternative to add value to these residues (Fermoso et al., 2018).
In the other scenarios (S1, S2 and S3) the sludge generated is stored for later application to the soil (Fig. 3), which reflects another important

Fig. 3. Daily mass flow as a function of the technical performance a) TS; b) VS; c) NH3-N; d) K and f) TOC; for the four scenarios (S1, S2, S3 and S4) in relation to the solid fraction. L1-Sieve sludge; L2-Settling tank sludge; L3-CLB sludge; L4-CSTR sludge; L5-N Module sludge. L6-P Module sludge.

aspect, because with the use of these treatment systems the management of this material is facilitated, the scenarios S1 and S3 end up having a lower sludge generation, the required agricultural area (3.77 m² m for S1 and 8.68 m² m for S3) to support the nutrient load (estimated as a function of P concentration as presented in the SM) is lower when compared to sce- narios S2 and S4 (12 m² m and 11.5 m² m respectively), it is worth noting that the CRST sludge is rich in nutrients due to the previous SLS step, concentrating the highest contents of P and K, which is favorable for the development of various crops (Arango-Osorio et al., 2019).

For the S4 the possibility of reusing the effluent resulting from the liquid route was also considered, that is, the output of the P module as water for washing the facilities, a process made possible due to the efficiency of the treatment that generates an effluent with low concentrations of pollutants and biosafety. Since the pH elevation in the P module allows the inactiva- tion of pathogenic microorganisms, as discussed by Cândido et al. (2022) and Viancelli et al. (2015), than in the final effluent there was no presence of biomarkers such as Salmonella, E. coli e Porcine circovirus type 2. Matsuzaki et al. (2021) point out that Ca(OH)2 is a powerful disinfectant due to its ability to raise the alkalinity of the medium and consequently in- activate these microorganisms. Moreover, another point that corroborates this reuse practice is that presented by Bortoli et al. (2022), according to the authors the residual concentrations of NO2 and NO3 present in these effluents allow reducing the methanogenesis of waste in the gutters and promote denitrification mitigating the emissions of harmful gases to the en- vironment. It is worth mentioning that although this point was explored only for the S4, all other scenarios present the capacity for water reuse, since the inactivation principle is the increase in pH.

Still from the energy balance data (Table 1), it is possible to verify that the systems manage to supply the energy demand, having an energy surplus 3.42 times greater than the consumption in S1, 1.69 times greater than the consumption in S2, 3.13 times greater than the consumption in S3 and 1.51 times greater than the consumption in S4. The improvement of the system in these scenarios is noticeable in all the processes of the system, not only in energy aspects, the motivation of improvement of the nitrogen removal process does not justify the removal of the AD process from the liquid frac- tion since the issue of absence of carbon for denitrification can be easily solved with a by-pass. Thus, although all processes are technologically fea- sible, since in the end they can promote an efficient treatment, the scenarios with energy use of the liquid fraction should be prioritized.

(2021), the volumetric ratio was 3.50E-02 Msludge Mmanure In addition to compromising the SLS process, the management of effluents in production facilities promotes the loss of GHG, especially methane, due to the degradation process that begins in the storage pits, reducing the potential of this waste and contributing to the GHG emissions (Im et al., 2020).

The second stage of SLS via sedimentation, the considered removal effi- ciencies (Table 1.3a of the SM) were distinct once it was identified that the management of the real scale system (S1) affected the process efficiency. Thus, for this scenario the removal efficiencies are 33 % for TS, % for total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN), 43 % for P, 7 % for the removal of TOC, besides sludge the generation compared to the initial volume about 10 While for the other scenarios (S2, S3 and S4), removal values were adopted of 60 % for TS, 2% for TAN, 80 for P, 30% for TOC. These initial stages are fundamental for functioning good operation of the subsequent units, a loss in efficiency of these processes is indicative of problems with effluent management, either within the production units, sludge manage- ment of the separation units, or in terms of overload, such as flows rates that exceed the design ones compromising the entire treatment (Vanotti et al., 2018).

In relation the Bio module, the divergences in the configurations pre- sented impacts on the systems not only in terms of biogas production, but also in its composition. The S1 presents satisfactory values in terms of bio- gas production, with 7.85 Mmanure, 76 % coming from the CLBs (0.56 Nmbiogas kgsv1), that is, the liquid fraction, and 24 % from the CSTR (0.38 Nmbiogas kgsv) that is, the solid fraction. This production results in an electrical energy generation of 1.24 kWh Nmbiogas, with an estimated heat cogeneration of 84.76 MJ Nmbiogas (Cândido et al., 2022).

The S2 have only the biogas generated from the solids system, which result in a production of 5.92 mmanure kgsv1), the value is higher than the reference scenario due to the higher efficiency of the sedimentation process that generates a sludge with a higher content of VS, in addition, the heat cogeneration is estimated at 63.97 MJ Nmbiogas-

For the S3 the liquid fraction is directed to four UASBs, reactors which leads to the production of a more energetically rich biogas, that is, with higher methane contents (around 75 %) (Zhao et al., 2021). Rico et al. (2017) highlighted that the use of UASB type reactors for the treatment of swine waste is an attractive alternative, according to the authors the combi- nation of lower hydraulic retention time (HRT), with a high organic loading rate (OLR), provides a better methanogenic activity that reflects in the rate of volumetric production of methane, resulting in a biogas with up to 80 of CH4, that is a very important point for energy aspect of utilization. Added to this, the better efficiency of the SLS, results in a biogas production of 7.34 Nmbiogas Mmanure, being, 32 % of the UASB (0.70 and 68 % of the CSTR (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsv1), and the electrical generation considered of 1.38 kWh Nmbiogas with 79.32 MJ Nmbiogas of cogenerated heat.

Technical performance for TS (a), VS (b), TAN (c), P (d), K (e) and TOC (f) for the four scenarios (S1, S2, S3 and S4) along the proposed treatment systems compared (P1 to P6) in relation to the liquid fraction. C = TSo: 16380 mg L-1; VS0: 11440 mg L-1; NH3-No: 1293.6 mg L-1 P0: 462.64 mg L-1; K0: 757.35 mg L-1; TOC: 3585.37 mg L-1.

In S4 only the sludge is used energetically by means of AD, but in this scenario the sludge resulting from N module is not directed to the CSTR re- actor reducing by half the input flow rate, but practically doubling the VS concentration, with this the biogas production was estimated at 5.30 Mmanure (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsu1 already the heat cogeneration was 57.28 MJ Nmbiogas

The improvement in the flow of solids directly affects the strategy of en- ergy use, since the scenarios with the absence of the reactor for the use of the liquid fraction (S2 and S4), do not have such significant losses com- pared to the other scenarios due to the better existing management. Favor- ing the digestion of solids through the CSTR reactor is important to the system not only to ensure the life of the CLB type reactors (present in S1) or UASB type (present in S3), but also because of the reduction of the oscil- lations of gas production due to the year seasonality effect, the cold months tend to reduce the gas production in reactors that do not have a heating system (Yan et al., 2022). Schmidt et al. (2019) found that the biogas pro- duction was affected by 30 % with the reduction of 7 °C in the system, compromising the stability of the process and the methane production. Thus, promoting efficient separation and the use of sludge reactors ensures that the solids will have a degradation with less interference all year round, maximized the biogas productivity of the plant, reducing the need for more reactor units.

After the AD, the effluent is directed to the N module. All scenarios pres- ent operational potentials with the conditions imposed at the module inlet. For this process one of the main factors that impact the different scenarios is the bioavailability of organic carbon at the system influent (Hollas et al., 2019). Since in the absence or insufficient supply of organic matter, the de- nitrification process occurs incompletely and can cause damage in terms of reducing the buffering potential, reducing the system pH and compromising the removal efficiency (Lee et al., 2019; Shi et al., 2020). Besides being potentially dangerous to the environment due to the higher probability of nitrous oxide (N20) losses from incomplete denitrification, since N2O is one of the intermediates of the process (Bortoli et al., 2022; Dai et al., 2021).

In the scenarios where the previous step was AD (S1 and S3), the carbon input is limited, since it was previously consumed by the AD process, thus in hot months where the efficiency of the Bio module is above average, there is a risk of the N removal system collapsing if organic matter supplementa- tion is not done (Wang et al., 2018). With the reduction in denitrification and the consequent decrease in pH, the presence of nitrite must be carefully monitored SO that inhibition by nitrous acid does not affect the system (Jiang et al., 2019). However this can be easily solved by diverting a frac- tion of the Bio module's input flow, ensuring the operation of the N module, as demonstrated by Awad et al. (2022), who found that increasing the carbon supply in the anoxic zone by means of a by-pass improved TN removal efficiency by about %.

For S2 and S4, the high availability of carbon guarantees the efficient functioning of N module, however the increase of dissolved organic carbon and the fraction of solids in the effluent that enters the system causes another problem, related to sludge generation (Table 1.5a of the SM). The higher carbon bioavailability favors the growth of heterotrophic organisms, which translates into a high generation of sludge, besides increasing the oxygen demand of the system, either through the difficulty in mass transfer, due to the higher content of solids, or by the demand for oxidation of these organic compounds (Steuernagel et al., 2018). Consequently the energy ex- penditure of the system is high (Table 1), being considered about 2 times higher than the reference scenario. Wang et al. (2021) reported that nitrifi- cation was severely compromised by the excess of organic compounds, also affecting the flocculation and sedimentation capacity of the nitrifying bio- mass, which adds to the generated sludge management problem (W. M. et al., 2020).

The high generation of sludge in the N module can be considered a problem beyond the energy aspect, since this waste does not present high fertilizing power (Fig. 3), nor even high potential for biogas generation (75 + 8 mLNCH4 gvs ). In S2, where the surplus sludge from N module is directed to the CSTR, this sludge ends up diluting the solids concentration up to 49 %, without having large contributions in biogas generation,

increasing the heating system demand about 1.7 times compared to the other scenarios. The high volume of sludge presents a high cost of treat- ment, according to Domini et al. (2022) for the year 2021, the average price of sludge treatment in Northern Italy averaged 176.9 ton consid- ering loading, transport, recovery/disposal and analysis costs. Not promot- ing the generation of excess sludge in the systems should be a priority since it has techno-economic consequences in the treatment plants. Thus, the elimination of the AD systems predecessors to this module can bring losses to the process operation.

P module the effluent characteristics resulted from N module will directly reflect on the performance of the P precipitation process (Table 1.6a of the SM). In S1 the Ca(OH)2 demand is about 4.5 times lower than for S2, because the higher alkalinity present in the effluent resulting from N module in S2, has a higher buffer capacity requiring more Ca(OH)2 for pH adjustment to the ideal precipitation conditions. Also the more and impure sludge is generated due to the remained TOC concentration that is coprecipitated. Christensen et al. (2022) studied the effects of organic matter on P precipita- tion and found a relatively large difference in the amount of precipitate (>50% for the liquid sample with a low carbon content compared to the other samples with a higher organic matter content, which also reflected in a precipitate with 20 % organic materials in the composition.

In S3 and S4 the precipitation of K-struvite was simulated to promote the removal of K, since this nutrient has no effective removal in any of the treatment modules. Theoretically equimolar ratios of K:P:Mg would be sufficient to promote K-struvite precipitation, so as this assumption was met in all scenarios, this process was simulated with the addition of NaOH in the effluent up to pH 9 (Le et al., 2021). With this, the NaOH de- mand would be 0.17 kg for S3 and 0.50 for S4, according to the test performed (described in 5.A in the SM). This higher demand in S4 is due to the same reason as S2, due to the higher alkalinity of the efflu- ent and consequent buffering of the medium. Although theoretically the ratios of K:P:Mg meet the premises of the process, the presence of Ca and residual NH3-N in the effluent, possibly would not promote the effective precipitation of K-struvite, but the precipitation of calcium phosphate or struvite, not removing K as expected. Furthermore, the ratio of K is about 10 times higher than the ratios of Mg and P, thus, supplementation of P and Mg in the effluent would be necessary to promote K removal.

Kabdasli et al. (2022) discussed that the process of K-struvite precipita- tion at stoichiometric doses can only be used for partial recovery of K as simulated in the present study, the authors verified the removal of about 40% of potassium, at equimolar ratios at pH values of 8 to 10, the improve- ment in removal efficiency was obtained when the system was overdosed with 100 % Mg and P and a pH of 10 resulting in 85 % recovery of K. With this, the simulated pH of 9 would possibly not be enough to favor ef- ficient K-struvite precipitation, which would demand more reagents for the process raising treatment costs. Thus, this alternative of P and K recovery, by the addition of NaOH was not configured as an effective way to jointly remove K and P from the effluent in the process under study.

S4 presents an additional step, related to the management of final sludge through composting. Although these sludges are generally stabi- lized, once they were submitted to the AD process, composting would act as a technology to reduce the humidity of these effluents, facilitating the ex- port/storage of this material (Sharara and Sadaka, 2018). At this stage, if the premises of the composting process are met, the process would occur ef- ficiently, which would result in the generation of a compost with 50 less volume and with a concentration of 0.13% of total nitrogen (TN), 0.12% of TOC, 0.12 9 of K and 0.14% of P. These values can still be im- proved, Z. Zhang et al. (2021) performed a meta-analysis and verified that the adjustment of the initial properties of the substrate, combined with control of the conditions of the composting process and even the appli- cation of additives can promote an improvement in the process and contrib- ute to the generation of a compost with better fertilizing quality. With this, there is the possibility of exploring this process as an alternative to add value to these residues (Fermoso et al., 2018).
In the other scenarios (S1, S2 and S3) the sludge generated is stored for later application to the soil (Fig. 3), which reflects another important

Fig. 3. Daily mass flow as a function of the technical performance a) TS; b) VS; c) NH3-N; d) K and f) TOC; for the four scenarios (S1, S2, S3 and S4) in relation to the solid fraction. L1-Sieve sludge; L2-Settling tank sludge; L3-CLB sludge; L4-CSTR sludge; L5-N Module sludge. L6-P Module sludge.

aspect, because with the use of these treatment systems the management of this material is facilitated, the scenarios S1 and S3 end up having a lower sludge generation, the required agricultural area (3.77 m² m for S1 and 8.68 m² m for S3) to support the nutrient load (estimated as a function of P concentration as presented in the SM) is lower when compared to sce- narios S2 and S4 (12 m² m and 11.5 m² m respectively), it is worth noting that the CRST sludge is rich in nutrients due to the previous SLS step, concentrating the highest contents of P and K, which is favorable for the development of various crops (Arango-Osorio et al., 2019).

For the S4 the possibility of reusing the effluent resulting from the liquid route was also considered, that is, the output of the P module as water for washing the facilities, a process made possible due to the efficiency of the treatment that generates an effluent with low concentrations of pollutants and biosafety. Since the pH elevation in the P module allows the inactiva- tion of pathogenic microorganisms, as discussed by Cândido et al. (2022) and Viancelli et al. (2015), than in the final effluent there was no presence of biomarkers such as Salmonella, E. coli e Porcine circovirus type 2. Matsuzaki et al. (2021) point out that Ca(OH)2 is a powerful disinfectant due to its ability to raise the alkalinity of the medium and consequently in- activate these microorganisms. Moreover, another point that corroborates this reuse practice is that presented by Bortoli et al. (2022), according to the authors the residual concentrations of NO2 and NO3 present in these effluents allow reducing the methanogenesis of waste in the gutters and promote denitrification mitigating the emissions of harmful gases to the en- vironment. It is worth mentioning that although this point was explored only for the S4, all other scenarios present the capacity for water reuse, since the inactivation principle is the increase in pH.

Still from the energy balance data (Table 1), it is possible to verify that the systems manage to supply the energy demand, having an energy surplus 3.42 times greater than the consumption in S1, 1.69 times greater than the consumption in S2, 3.13 times greater than the consumption in S3 and 1.51 times greater than the consumption in S4. The improvement of the system in these scenarios is noticeable in all the processes of the system, not only in energy aspects, the motivation of improvement of the nitrogen removal process does not justify the removal of the AD process from the liquid frac- tion since the issue of absence of carbon for denitrification can be easily solved with a by-pass. Thus, although all processes are technologically fea- sible, since in the end they can promote an efficient treatment, the scenarios with energy use of the liquid fraction should be prioritized.
37: The technical performance of the different scenarios under study high- lighted how changes in treatment system configurations affected the

individual processes in a cascading effect. In addition, all scenarios showed applicability, with minor issues that favored or worsened the results of each process. In the first stage of the systems, i.e., SLS by screening, similar values in terms of separation efficiency were adopted in all scenarios based on the values of the reference scenario (S1), 12 % efficiency for total solids (TS) removal, % for phosphorus (P) removal, 2 % for total ni- trogen (TN) removal and 8 % for total organic carbon (TOC) removal (Table 1.3a of the SM) (Fig. 2). These values are consistent with average values in the literature, such as those presented by Amaral et al. (2016), who studied the effect of SLS on biogas generation capacity for swine ma- nure, and screening efficiency that ranged from 6 % to 21 %.

In S1 the Ca(OH)2 demand is about 4.5 times lower than for S2, because the higher alkalinity present in the effluent resulting from N module in S2, has a higher buffer capacity requiring more Ca(OH)2 for pH adjustment to the ideal precipitation conditions. Also the more and impure sludge is generated due to the remained TOC concentration that is coprecipitated. Christensen et al. (2022) studied the effects of organic matter on P precipita- tion and found a relatively large difference in the amount of precipitate (>50% for the liquid sample with a low carbon content compared to the other samples with a higher organic matter content, which also reflected in a precipitate with 20 % organic materials in the composition.

In S3 and S4 the precipitation of K-struvite was simulated to promote the removal of K, since this nutrient has no effective removal in any of the treatment modules. Theoretically equimolar ratios of K:P:Mg would be sufficient to promote K-struvite precipitation, so as this assumption was met in all scenarios, this process was simulated with the addition of NaOH in the effluent up to pH 9 (Le et al., 2021). With this, the NaOH de- mand would be 0.17 kg for S3 and 0.50 for S4, according to the test performed (described in 5.A in the SM). This higher demand in S4 is due to the same reason as S2, due to the higher alkalinity of the efflu- ent and consequent buffering of the medium. Although theoretically the ratios of K:P:Mg meet the premises of the process, the presence of Ca and residual NH3-N in the effluent, possibly would not promote the effective precipitation of K-struvite, but the precipitation of calcium phosphate or struvite, not removing K as expected. Furthermore, the ratio of K is about 10 times higher than the ratios of Mg and P, thus, supplementation of P and Mg in the effluent would be necessary to promote K removal.

Kabdasli et al. (2022) discussed that the process of K-struvite precipita- tion at stoichiometric doses can only be used for partial recovery of K as simulated in the present study, the authors verified the removal of about 40% of potassium, at equimolar ratios at pH values of 8 to 10, the improve- ment in removal efficiency was obtained when the system was overdosed with 100 % Mg and P and a pH of 10 resulting in 85 % recovery of K. With this, the simulated pH of 9 would possibly not be enough to favor ef- ficient K-struvite precipitation, which would demand more reagents for the process raising treatment costs. Thus, this alternative of P and K recovery, by the addition of NaOH was not configured as an effective way to jointly remove K and P from the effluent in the process under study.

S4 presents an additional step, related to the management of final sludge through composting. Although these sludges are generally stabi- lized, once they were submitted to the AD process, composting would act as a technology to reduce the humidity of these effluents, facilitating the ex- port/storage of this material (Sharara and Sadaka, 2018). At this stage, if the premises of the composting process are met, the process would occur ef- ficiently, which would result in the generation of a compost with 50 less volume and with a concentration of 0.13% of total nitrogen (TN), 0.12% of TOC, 0.12 9 of K and 0.14% of P. These values can still be im- proved, Z. Zhang et al. (2021) performed a meta-analysis and verified that the adjustment of the initial properties of the substrate, combined with control of the conditions of the composting process and even the appli- cation of additives can promote an improvement in the process and contrib- ute to the generation of a compost with better fertilizing quality. With this, there is the possibility of exploring this process as an alternative to add value to these residues (Fermoso et al., 2018).
In the other scenarios (S1, S2 and S3) the sludge generated is stored for later application to the soil (Fig. 3), which reflects another important

Fig. 3. Daily mass flow as a function of the technical performance a) TS; b) VS; c) NH3-N; d) K and f) TOC; for the four scenarios (S1, S2, S3 and S4) in relation to the solid fraction. L1-Sieve sludge; L2-Settling tank sludge; L3-CLB sludge; L4-CSTR sludge; L5-N Module sludge. L6-P Module sludge.

aspect, because with the use of these treatment systems the management of this material is facilitated, the scenarios S1 and S3 end up having a lower sludge generation, the required agricultural area (3.77 m² m for S1 and 8.68 m² m for S3) to support the nutrient load (estimated as a function of P concentration as presented in the SM) is lower when compared to sce- narios S2 and S4 (12 m² m and 11.5 m² m respectively), it is worth noting that the CRST sludge is rich in nutrients due to the previous SLS step, concentrating the highest contents of P and K, which is favorable for the development of various crops (Arango-Osorio et al., 2019).

For the S4 the possibility of reusing the effluent resulting from the liquid route was also considered, that is, the output of the P module as water for washing the facilities, a process made possible due to the efficiency of the treatment that generates an effluent with low concentrations of pollutants and biosafety. Since the pH elevation in the P module allows the inactiva- tion of pathogenic microorganisms, as discussed by Cândido et al. (2022) and Viancelli et al. (2015), than in the final effluent there was no presence of biomarkers such as Salmonella, E. coli e Porcine circovirus type 2. Matsuzaki et al. (2021) point out that Ca(OH)2 is a powerful disinfectant due to its ability to raise the alkalinity of the medium and consequently in- activate these microorganisms. Moreover, another point that corroborates this reuse practice is that presented by Bortoli et al. (2022), according to the authors the residual concentrations of NO2 and NO3 present in these effluents allow reducing the methanogenesis of waste in the gutters and promote denitrification mitigating the emissions of harmful gases to the en- vironment. It is worth mentioning that although this point was explored only for the S4, all other scenarios present the capacity for water reuse, since the inactivation principle is the increase in pH.

Still from the energy balance data (Table 1), it is possible to verify that the systems manage to supply the energy demand, having an energy surplus 3.42 times greater than the consumption in S1, 1.69 times greater than the consumption in S2, 3.13 times greater than the consumption in S3 and 1.51 times greater than the consumption in S4. The improvement of the system in these scenarios is noticeable in all the processes of the system, not only in energy aspects, the motivation of improvement of the nitrogen removal process does not justify the removal of the AD process from the liquid frac- tion since the issue of absence of carbon for denitrification can be easily solved with a by-pass. Thus, although all processes are technologically fea- sible, since in the end they can promote an efficient treatment, the scenarios with energy use of the liquid fraction should be prioritized.
38: The technical performance of the different scenarios under study high- lighted how changes in treatment system configurations affected the

individual processes in a cascading effect. In addition, all scenarios showed applicability, with minor issues that favored or worsened the results of each process. In the first stage of the systems, i.e., SLS by screening, similar values in terms of separation efficiency were adopted in all scenarios based on the values of the reference scenario (S1), 12 % efficiency for total solids (TS) removal, % for phosphorus (P) removal, 2 % for total ni- trogen (TN) removal and 8 % for total organic carbon (TOC) removal (Table 1.3a of the SM) (Fig. 2). These values are consistent with average values in the literature, such as those presented by Amaral et al. (2016), who studied the effect of SLS on biogas generation capacity for swine ma- nure, and screening efficiency that ranged from 6 % to 21 %.

However, a preliminary study (Hollas et al., 2021) found average solids removal efficiencies around 30 % for screening, emphasizing the effect of manure management, especially in terms of storage time, within the facili- ties as a critical factor in the process of solids removal via mechanical de- vices. The management inside the animal production facilities also affects the process efficiency, due to this, the volumes of sludge generated were different in the scenarios, being the ratio of 7.40E-03 Msludge Mmanure for S1 (Table 1.7a of the SM), while for S2, S3 and S4 considering an ideal man- agement, as proposed by Hollas et al. (2021), the volumetric ratio was 3.50E-02 Msludge Mmanure In addition to compromising the SLS process, the management of effluents in production facilities promotes the loss of GHG, especially methane, due to the degradation process that begins in the storage pits, reducing the potential of this waste and contributing to the GHG emissions (Im et al., 2020).

The second stage of SLS via sedimentation, the considered removal effi- ciencies (Table 1.3a of the SM) were distinct once it was identified that the management of the real scale system (S1) affected the process efficiency. Thus, for this scenario the removal efficiencies are 33 % for TS, % for total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN), 43 % for P, 7 % for the removal of TOC, besides sludge the generation compared to the initial volume about 10 While for the other scenarios (S2, S3 and S4), removal values were adopted of 60 % for TS, 2% for TAN, 80 for P, 30% for TOC. These initial stages are fundamental for functioning good operation of the subsequent units, a loss in efficiency of these processes is indicative of problems with effluent management, either within the production units, sludge manage- ment of the separation units, or in terms of overload, such as flows rates that exceed the design ones compromising the entire treatment (Vanotti et al., 2018).

In relation the Bio module, the divergences in the configurations pre- sented impacts on the systems not only in terms of biogas production, but also in its composition. The S1 presents satisfactory values in terms of bio- gas production, with 7.85 Mmanure, 76 % coming from the CLBs (0.56 Nmbiogas kgsv1), that is, the liquid fraction, and 24 % from the CSTR (0.38 Nmbiogas kgsv) that is, the solid fraction. This production results in an electrical energy generation of 1.24 kWh Nmbiogas, with an estimated heat cogeneration of 84.76 MJ Nmbiogas (Cândido et al., 2022).

The S2 have only the biogas generated from the solids system, which result in a production of 5.92 mmanure kgsv1), the value is higher than the reference scenario due to the higher efficiency of the sedimentation process that generates a sludge with a higher content of VS, in addition, the heat cogeneration is estimated at 63.97 MJ Nmbiogas-

For the S3 the liquid fraction is directed to four UASBs, reactors which leads to the production of a more energetically rich biogas, that is, with higher methane contents (around 75 %) (Zhao et al., 2021). Rico et al. (2017) highlighted that the use of UASB type reactors for the treatment of swine waste is an attractive alternative, according to the authors the combi- nation of lower hydraulic retention time (HRT), with a high organic loading rate (OLR), provides a better methanogenic activity that reflects in the rate of volumetric production of methane, resulting in a biogas with up to 80 of CH4, that is a very important point for energy aspect of utilization. Added to this, the better efficiency of the SLS, results in a biogas production of 7.34 Nmbiogas Mmanure, being, 32 % of the UASB (0.70 and 68 % of the CSTR (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsv1), and the electrical generation considered of 1.38 kWh Nmbiogas with 79.32 MJ Nmbiogas of cogenerated heat.
