(2021) reported that nitrifi- cation was severely compromised by the excess of organic compounds, also affecting the flocculation and sedimentation capacity of the nitrifying bio- mass, which adds to the generated sludge management problem (W. M. et al., 2020).

The high generation of sludge in the N module can be considered a problem beyond the energy aspect, since this waste does not present high fertilizing power (Fig. 3), nor even high potential for biogas generation (75 + 8 mLNCH4 gvs ). In S2, where the surplus sludge from N module is directed to the CSTR, this sludge ends up diluting the solids concentration up to 49 %, without having large contributions in biogas generation,

increasing the heating system demand about 1.7 times compared to the other scenarios. The high volume of sludge presents a high cost of treat- ment, according to Domini et al. (2022) for the year 2021, the average price of sludge treatment in Northern Italy averaged 176.9 ton consid- ering loading, transport, recovery/disposal and analysis costs. Not promot- ing the generation of excess sludge in the systems should be a priority since it has techno-economic consequences in the treatment plants. Thus, the elimination of the AD systems predecessors to this module can bring losses to the process operation.

P module the effluent characteristics resulted from N module will directly reflect on the performance of the P precipitation process (Table 1.6a of the SM). In S1 the Ca(OH)2 demand is about 4.5 times lower than for S2, because the higher alkalinity present in the effluent resulting from N module in S2, has a higher buffer capacity requiring more Ca(OH)2 for pH adjustment to the ideal precipitation conditions. Also the more and impure sludge is generated due to the remained TOC concentration that is coprecipitated. Christensen et al. (2022) studied the effects of organic matter on P precipita- tion and found a relatively large difference in the amount of precipitate (>50% for the liquid sample with a low carbon content compared to the other samples with a higher organic matter content, which also reflected in a precipitate with 20 % organic materials in the composition.

In S3 and S4 the precipitation of K-struvite was simulated to promote the removal of K, since this nutrient has no effective removal in any of the treatment modules. Theoretically equimolar ratios of K:P:Mg would be sufficient to promote K-struvite precipitation, so as this assumption was met in all scenarios, this process was simulated with the addition of NaOH in the effluent up to pH 9 (Le et al., 2021). With this, the NaOH de- mand would be 0.17 kg for S3 and 0.50 for S4, according to the test performed (described in 5.A in the SM). This higher demand in S4 is due to the same reason as S2, due to the higher alkalinity of the efflu- ent and consequent buffering of the medium. Although theoretically the ratios of K:P:Mg meet the premises of the process, the presence of Ca and residual NH3-N in the effluent, possibly would not promote the effective precipitation of K-struvite, but the precipitation of calcium phosphate or struvite, not removing K as expected. Furthermore, the ratio of K is about 10 times higher than the ratios of Mg and P, thus, supplementation of P and Mg in the effluent would be necessary to promote K removal.

Kabdasli et al. (2022) discussed that the process of K-struvite precipita- tion at stoichiometric doses can only be used for partial recovery of K as simulated in the present study, the authors verified the removal of about 40% of potassium, at equimolar ratios at pH values of 8 to 10, the improve- ment in removal efficiency was obtained when the system was overdosed with 100 % Mg and P and a pH of 10 resulting in 85 % recovery of K. With this, the simulated pH of 9 would possibly not be enough to favor ef- ficient K-struvite precipitation, which would demand more reagents for the process raising treatment costs. Thus, this alternative of P and K recovery, by the addition of NaOH was not configured as an effective way to jointly remove K and P from the effluent in the process under study.

S4 presents an additional step, related to the management of final sludge through composting. Although these sludges are generally stabi- lized, once they were submitted to the AD process, composting would act as a technology to reduce the humidity of these effluents, facilitating the ex- port/storage of this material (Sharara and Sadaka, 2018). At this stage, if the premises of the composting process are met, the process would occur ef- ficiently, which would result in the generation of a compost with 50 less volume and with a concentration of 0.13% of total nitrogen (TN), 0.12% of TOC, 0.12 9 of K and 0.14% of P. These values can still be im- proved, Z. Zhang et al. (2021) performed a meta-analysis and verified that the adjustment of the initial properties of the substrate, combined with control of the conditions of the composting process and even the appli- cation of additives can promote an improvement in the process and contrib- ute to the generation of a compost with better fertilizing quality. With this, there is the possibility of exploring this process as an alternative to add value to these residues (Fermoso et al., 2018).
In the other scenarios (S1, S2 and S3) the sludge generated is stored for later application to the soil (Fig. 3), which reflects another important

Fig. 3. Daily mass flow as a function of the technical performance a) TS; b) VS; c) NH3-N; d) K and f) TOC; for the four scenarios (S1, S2, S3 and S4) in relation to the solid fraction. L1-Sieve sludge; L2-Settling tank sludge; L3-CLB sludge; L4-CSTR sludge; L5-N Module sludge. L6-P Module sludge.

aspect, because with the use of these treatment systems the management of this material is facilitated, the scenarios S1 and S3 end up having a lower sludge generation, the required agricultural area (3.77 m² m for S1 and 8.68 m² m for S3) to support the nutrient load (estimated as a function of P concentration as presented in the SM) is lower when compared to sce- narios S2 and S4 (12 m² m and 11.5 m² m respectively), it is worth noting that the CRST sludge is rich in nutrients due to the previous SLS step, concentrating the highest contents of P and K, which is favorable for the development of various crops (Arango-Osorio et al., 2019).

For the S4 the possibility of reusing the effluent resulting from the liquid route was also considered, that is, the output of the P module as water for washing the facilities, a process made possible due to the efficiency of the treatment that generates an effluent with low concentrations of pollutants and biosafety. Since the pH elevation in the P module allows the inactiva- tion of pathogenic microorganisms, as discussed by Cândido et al. (2022) and Viancelli et al. (2015), than in the final effluent there was no presence of biomarkers such as Salmonella, E. coli e Porcine circovirus type 2. Matsuzaki et al. (2021) point out that Ca(OH)2 is a powerful disinfectant due to its ability to raise the alkalinity of the medium and consequently in- activate these microorganisms. Moreover, another point that corroborates this reuse practice is that presented by Bortoli et al. (2022), according to the authors the residual concentrations of NO2 and NO3 present in these effluents allow reducing the methanogenesis of waste in the gutters and promote denitrification mitigating the emissions of harmful gases to the en- vironment. It is worth mentioning that although this point was explored only for the S4, all other scenarios present the capacity for water reuse, since the inactivation principle is the increase in pH.

Still from the energy balance data (Table 1), it is possible to verify that the systems manage to supply the energy demand, having an energy surplus 3.42 times greater than the consumption in S1, 1.69 times greater than the consumption in S2, 3.13 times greater than the consumption in S3 and 1.51 times greater than the consumption in S4. The improvement of the system in these scenarios is noticeable in all the processes of the system, not only in energy aspects, the motivation of improvement of the nitrogen removal process does not justify the removal of the AD process from the liquid frac- tion since the issue of absence of carbon for denitrification can be easily solved with a by-pass. Thus, although all processes are technologically fea- sible, since in the end they can promote an efficient treatment, the scenarios with energy use of the liquid fraction should be prioritized.
46: The technical performance of the different scenarios under study high- lighted how changes in treatment system configurations affected the

individual processes in a cascading effect. In addition, all scenarios showed applicability, with minor issues that favored or worsened the results of each process. In the first stage of the systems, i.e., SLS by screening, similar values in terms of separation efficiency were adopted in all scenarios based on the values of the reference scenario (S1), 12 % efficiency for total solids (TS) removal, % for phosphorus (P) removal, 2 % for total ni- trogen (TN) removal and 8 % for total organic carbon (TOC) removal (Table 1.3a of the SM) (Fig. 2). These values are consistent with average values in the literature, such as those presented by Amaral et al. (2016), who studied the effect of SLS on biogas generation capacity for swine ma- nure, and screening efficiency that ranged from 6 % to 21 %.

However, a preliminary study (Hollas et al., 2021) found average solids removal efficiencies around 30 % for screening, emphasizing the effect of manure management, especially in terms of storage time, within the facili- ties as a critical factor in the process of solids removal via mechanical de- vices. The management inside the animal production facilities also affects the process efficiency, due to this, the volumes of sludge generated were different in the scenarios, being the ratio of 7.40E-03 Msludge Mmanure for S1 (Table 1.7a of the SM), while for S2, S3 and S4 considering an ideal man- agement, as proposed by Hollas et al. (2021), the volumetric ratio was 3.50E-02 Msludge Mmanure In addition to compromising the SLS process, the management of effluents in production facilities promotes the loss of GHG, especially methane, due to the degradation process that begins in the storage pits, reducing the potential of this waste and contributing to the GHG emissions (Im et al., 2020).

The second stage of SLS via sedimentation, the considered removal effi- ciencies (Table 1.3a of the SM) were distinct once it was identified that the management of the real scale system (S1) affected the process efficiency. Thus, for this scenario the removal efficiencies are 33 % for TS, % for total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN), 43 % for P, 7 % for the removal of TOC, besides sludge the generation compared to the initial volume about 10 While for the other scenarios (S2, S3 and S4), removal values were adopted of 60 % for TS, 2% for TAN, 80 for P, 30% for TOC. These initial stages are fundamental for functioning good operation of the subsequent units, a loss in efficiency of these processes is indicative of problems with effluent management, either within the production units, sludge manage- ment of the separation units, or in terms of overload, such as flows rates that exceed the design ones compromising the entire treatment (Vanotti et al., 2018).

In relation the Bio module, the divergences in the configurations pre- sented impacts on the systems not only in terms of biogas production, but also in its composition. The S1 presents satisfactory values in terms of bio- gas production, with 7.85 Mmanure, 76 % coming from the CLBs (0.56 Nmbiogas kgsv1), that is, the liquid fraction, and 24 % from the CSTR (0.38 Nmbiogas kgsv) that is, the solid fraction. This production results in an electrical energy generation of 1.24 kWh Nmbiogas, with an estimated heat cogeneration of 84.76 MJ Nmbiogas (Cândido et al., 2022).

The S2 have only the biogas generated from the solids system, which result in a production of 5.92 mmanure kgsv1), the value is higher than the reference scenario due to the higher efficiency of the sedimentation process that generates a sludge with a higher content of VS, in addition, the heat cogeneration is estimated at 63.97 MJ Nmbiogas-

For the S3 the liquid fraction is directed to four UASBs, reactors which leads to the production of a more energetically rich biogas, that is, with higher methane contents (around 75 %) (Zhao et al., 2021). Rico et al. (2017) highlighted that the use of UASB type reactors for the treatment of swine waste is an attractive alternative, according to the authors the combi- nation of lower hydraulic retention time (HRT), with a high organic loading rate (OLR), provides a better methanogenic activity that reflects in the rate of volumetric production of methane, resulting in a biogas with up to 80 of CH4, that is a very important point for energy aspect of utilization. Added to this, the better efficiency of the SLS, results in a biogas production of 7.34 Nmbiogas Mmanure, being, 32 % of the UASB (0.70 and 68 % of the CSTR (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsv1), and the electrical generation considered of 1.38 kWh Nmbiogas with 79.32 MJ Nmbiogas of cogenerated heat.

In S4 only the sludge is used energetically by means of AD, but in this scenario the sludge resulting from N module is not directed to the CSTR re- actor reducing by half the input flow rate, but practically doubling the VS concentration, with this the biogas production was estimated at 5.30 Mmanure (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsu1 already the heat cogeneration was 57.28 MJ Nmbiogas

The improvement in the flow of solids directly affects the strategy of en- ergy use, since the scenarios with the absence of the reactor for the use of the liquid fraction (S2 and S4), do not have such significant losses com- pared to the other scenarios due to the better existing management. Favor- ing the digestion of solids through the CSTR reactor is important to the system not only to ensure the life of the CLB type reactors (present in S1) or UASB type (present in S3), but also because of the reduction of the oscil- lations of gas production due to the year seasonality effect, the cold months tend to reduce the gas production in reactors that do not have a heating system (Yan et al., 2022). Schmidt et al. (2019) found that the biogas pro- duction was affected by 30 % with the reduction of 7 °C in the system, compromising the stability of the process and the methane production. Thus, promoting efficient separation and the use of sludge reactors ensures that the solids will have a degradation with less interference all year round, maximized the biogas productivity of the plant, reducing the need for more reactor units.

After the AD, the effluent is directed to the N module. All scenarios pres- ent operational potentials with the conditions imposed at the module inlet. For this process one of the main factors that impact the different scenarios is the bioavailability of organic carbon at the system influent (Hollas et al., 2019). Since in the absence or insufficient supply of organic matter, the de- nitrification process occurs incompletely and can cause damage in terms of reducing the buffering potential, reducing the system pH and compromising the removal efficiency (Lee et al., 2019; Shi et al., 2020). Besides being potentially dangerous to the environment due to the higher probability of nitrous oxide (N20) losses from incomplete denitrification, since N2O is one of the intermediates of the process (Bortoli et al., 2022; Dai et al., 2021).

In the scenarios where the previous step was AD (S1 and S3), the carbon input is limited, since it was previously consumed by the AD process, thus in hot months where the efficiency of the Bio module is above average, there is a risk of the N removal system collapsing if organic matter supplementa- tion is not done (Wang et al., 2018). With the reduction in denitrification and the consequent decrease in pH, the presence of nitrite must be carefully monitored SO that inhibition by nitrous acid does not affect the system (Jiang et al., 2019). However this can be easily solved by diverting a frac- tion of the Bio module's input flow, ensuring the operation of the N module, as demonstrated by Awad et al. (2022), who found that increasing the carbon supply in the anoxic zone by means of a by-pass improved TN removal efficiency by about %.

For S2 and S4, the high availability of carbon guarantees the efficient functioning of N module, however the increase of dissolved organic carbon and the fraction of solids in the effluent that enters the system causes another problem, related to sludge generation (Table 1.5a of the SM). The higher carbon bioavailability favors the growth of heterotrophic organisms, which translates into a high generation of sludge, besides increasing the oxygen demand of the system, either through the difficulty in mass transfer, due to the higher content of solids, or by the demand for oxidation of these organic compounds (Steuernagel et al., 2018). Consequently the energy ex- penditure of the system is high (Table 1), being considered about 2 times higher than the reference scenario. Wang et al. (2021) reported that nitrifi- cation was severely compromised by the excess of organic compounds, also affecting the flocculation and sedimentation capacity of the nitrifying bio- mass, which adds to the generated sludge management problem (W. M. et al., 2020).

The high generation of sludge in the N module can be considered a problem beyond the energy aspect, since this waste does not present high fertilizing power (Fig. 3), nor even high potential for biogas generation (75 + 8 mLNCH4 gvs ). In S2, where the surplus sludge from N module is directed to the CSTR, this sludge ends up diluting the solids concentration up to 49 %, without having large contributions in biogas generation,

increasing the heating system demand about 1.7 times compared to the other scenarios. The high volume of sludge presents a high cost of treat- ment, according to Domini et al. (2022) for the year 2021, the average price of sludge treatment in Northern Italy averaged 176.9 ton consid- ering loading, transport, recovery/disposal and analysis costs. Not promot- ing the generation of excess sludge in the systems should be a priority since it has techno-economic consequences in the treatment plants. Thus, the elimination of the AD systems predecessors to this module can bring losses to the process operation.

For the S3 the liquid fraction is directed to four UASBs, reactors which leads to the production of a more energetically rich biogas, that is, with higher methane contents (around 75 %) (Zhao et al., 2021). Rico et al. (2017) highlighted that the use of UASB type reactors for the treatment of swine waste is an attractive alternative, according to the authors the combi- nation of lower hydraulic retention time (HRT), with a high organic loading rate (OLR), provides a better methanogenic activity that reflects in the rate of volumetric production of methane, resulting in a biogas with up to 80 of CH4, that is a very important point for energy aspect of utilization. Added to this, the better efficiency of the SLS, results in a biogas production of 7.34 Nmbiogas Mmanure, being, 32 % of the UASB (0.70 and 68 % of the CSTR (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsv1), and the electrical generation considered of 1.38 kWh Nmbiogas with 79.32 MJ Nmbiogas of cogenerated heat.

Technical performance for TS (a), VS (b), TAN (c), P (d), K (e) and TOC (f) for the four scenarios (S1, S2, S3 and S4) along the proposed treatment systems compared (P1 to P6) in relation to the liquid fraction. C = TSo: 16380 mg L-1; VS0: 11440 mg L-1; NH3-No: 1293.6 mg L-1 P0: 462.64 mg L-1; K0: 757.35 mg L-1; TOC: 3585.37 mg L-1.

In S4 only the sludge is used energetically by means of AD, but in this scenario the sludge resulting from N module is not directed to the CSTR re- actor reducing by half the input flow rate, but practically doubling the VS concentration, with this the biogas production was estimated at 5.30 Mmanure (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsu1 already the heat cogeneration was 57.28 MJ Nmbiogas

The improvement in the flow of solids directly affects the strategy of en- ergy use, since the scenarios with the absence of the reactor for the use of the liquid fraction (S2 and S4), do not have such significant losses com- pared to the other scenarios due to the better existing management. Favor- ing the digestion of solids through the CSTR reactor is important to the system not only to ensure the life of the CLB type reactors (present in S1) or UASB type (present in S3), but also because of the reduction of the oscil- lations of gas production due to the year seasonality effect, the cold months tend to reduce the gas production in reactors that do not have a heating system (Yan et al., 2022). Schmidt et al. (2019) found that the biogas pro- duction was affected by 30 % with the reduction of 7 °C in the system, compromising the stability of the process and the methane production. Thus, promoting efficient separation and the use of sludge reactors ensures that the solids will have a degradation with less interference all year round, maximized the biogas productivity of the plant, reducing the need for more reactor units.

After the AD, the effluent is directed to the N module. All scenarios pres- ent operational potentials with the conditions imposed at the module inlet. For this process one of the main factors that impact the different scenarios is the bioavailability of organic carbon at the system influent (Hollas et al., 2019). Since in the absence or insufficient supply of organic matter, the de- nitrification process occurs incompletely and can cause damage in terms of reducing the buffering potential, reducing the system pH and compromising the removal efficiency (Lee et al., 2019; Shi et al., 2020). Besides being potentially dangerous to the environment due to the higher probability of nitrous oxide (N20) losses from incomplete denitrification, since N2O is one of the intermediates of the process (Bortoli et al., 2022; Dai et al., 2021).

In the scenarios where the previous step was AD (S1 and S3), the carbon input is limited, since it was previously consumed by the AD process, thus in hot months where the efficiency of the Bio module is above average, there is a risk of the N removal system collapsing if organic matter supplementa- tion is not done (Wang et al., 2018). With the reduction in denitrification and the consequent decrease in pH, the presence of nitrite must be carefully monitored SO that inhibition by nitrous acid does not affect the system (Jiang et al., 2019). However this can be easily solved by diverting a frac- tion of the Bio module's input flow, ensuring the operation of the N module, as demonstrated by Awad et al. (2022), who found that increasing the carbon supply in the anoxic zone by means of a by-pass improved TN removal efficiency by about %.

For S2 and S4, the high availability of carbon guarantees the efficient functioning of N module, however the increase of dissolved organic carbon and the fraction of solids in the effluent that enters the system causes another problem, related to sludge generation (Table 1.5a of the SM). The higher carbon bioavailability favors the growth of heterotrophic organisms, which translates into a high generation of sludge, besides increasing the oxygen demand of the system, either through the difficulty in mass transfer, due to the higher content of solids, or by the demand for oxidation of these organic compounds (Steuernagel et al., 2018). Consequently the energy ex- penditure of the system is high (Table 1), being considered about 2 times higher than the reference scenario. Wang et al. (2021) reported that nitrifi- cation was severely compromised by the excess of organic compounds, also affecting the flocculation and sedimentation capacity of the nitrifying bio- mass, which adds to the generated sludge management problem (W. M. et al., 2020).

The high generation of sludge in the N module can be considered a problem beyond the energy aspect, since this waste does not present high fertilizing power (Fig. 3), nor even high potential for biogas generation (75 + 8 mLNCH4 gvs ). In S2, where the surplus sludge from N module is directed to the CSTR, this sludge ends up diluting the solids concentration up to 49 %, without having large contributions in biogas generation,

increasing the heating system demand about 1.7 times compared to the other scenarios. The high volume of sludge presents a high cost of treat- ment, according to Domini et al. (2022) for the year 2021, the average price of sludge treatment in Northern Italy averaged 176.9 ton consid- ering loading, transport, recovery/disposal and analysis costs. Not promot- ing the generation of excess sludge in the systems should be a priority since it has techno-economic consequences in the treatment plants. Thus, the elimination of the AD systems predecessors to this module can bring losses to the process operation.

P module the effluent characteristics resulted from N module will directly reflect on the performance of the P precipitation process (Table 1.6a of the SM). In S1 the Ca(OH)2 demand is about 4.5 times lower than for S2, because the higher alkalinity present in the effluent resulting from N module in S2, has a higher buffer capacity requiring more Ca(OH)2 for pH adjustment to the ideal precipitation conditions. Also the more and impure sludge is generated due to the remained TOC concentration that is coprecipitated. Christensen et al. (2022) studied the effects of organic matter on P precipita- tion and found a relatively large difference in the amount of precipitate (>50% for the liquid sample with a low carbon content compared to the other samples with a higher organic matter content, which also reflected in a precipitate with 20 % organic materials in the composition.

In S3 and S4 the precipitation of K-struvite was simulated to promote the removal of K, since this nutrient has no effective removal in any of the treatment modules. Theoretically equimolar ratios of K:P:Mg would be sufficient to promote K-struvite precipitation, so as this assumption was met in all scenarios, this process was simulated with the addition of NaOH in the effluent up to pH 9 (Le et al., 2021). With this, the NaOH de- mand would be 0.17 kg for S3 and 0.50 for S4, according to the test performed (described in 5.A in the SM). This higher demand in S4 is due to the same reason as S2, due to the higher alkalinity of the efflu- ent and consequent buffering of the medium. Although theoretically the ratios of K:P:Mg meet the premises of the process, the presence of Ca and residual NH3-N in the effluent, possibly would not promote the effective precipitation of K-struvite, but the precipitation of calcium phosphate or struvite, not removing K as expected. Furthermore, the ratio of K is about 10 times higher than the ratios of Mg and P, thus, supplementation of P and Mg in the effluent would be necessary to promote K removal.

Kabdasli et al. (2022) discussed that the process of K-struvite precipita- tion at stoichiometric doses can only be used for partial recovery of K as simulated in the present study, the authors verified the removal of about 40% of potassium, at equimolar ratios at pH values of 8 to 10, the improve- ment in removal efficiency was obtained when the system was overdosed with 100 % Mg and P and a pH of 10 resulting in 85 % recovery of K. With this, the simulated pH of 9 would possibly not be enough to favor ef- ficient K-struvite precipitation, which would demand more reagents for the process raising treatment costs. Thus, this alternative of P and K recovery, by the addition of NaOH was not configured as an effective way to jointly remove K and P from the effluent in the process under study.

S4 presents an additional step, related to the management of final sludge through composting. Although these sludges are generally stabi- lized, once they were submitted to the AD process, composting would act as a technology to reduce the humidity of these effluents, facilitating the ex- port/storage of this material (Sharara and Sadaka, 2018). At this stage, if the premises of the composting process are met, the process would occur ef- ficiently, which would result in the generation of a compost with 50 less volume and with a concentration of 0.13% of total nitrogen (TN), 0.12% of TOC, 0.12 9 of K and 0.14% of P. These values can still be im- proved, Z. Zhang et al. (2021) performed a meta-analysis and verified that the adjustment of the initial properties of the substrate, combined with control of the conditions of the composting process and even the appli- cation of additives can promote an improvement in the process and contrib- ute to the generation of a compost with better fertilizing quality. With this, there is the possibility of exploring this process as an alternative to add value to these residues (Fermoso et al., 2018).
In the other scenarios (S1, S2 and S3) the sludge generated is stored for later application to the soil (Fig. 3), which reflects another important

Fig. 3. Daily mass flow as a function of the technical performance a) TS; b) VS; c) NH3-N; d) K and f) TOC; for the four scenarios (S1, S2, S3 and S4) in relation to the solid fraction. L1-Sieve sludge; L2-Settling tank sludge; L3-CLB sludge; L4-CSTR sludge; L5-N Module sludge. L6-P Module sludge.

aspect, because with the use of these treatment systems the management of this material is facilitated, the scenarios S1 and S3 end up having a lower sludge generation, the required agricultural area (3.77 m² m for S1 and 8.68 m² m for S3) to support the nutrient load (estimated as a function of P concentration as presented in the SM) is lower when compared to sce- narios S2 and S4 (12 m² m and 11.5 m² m respectively), it is worth noting that the CRST sludge is rich in nutrients due to the previous SLS step, concentrating the highest contents of P and K, which is favorable for the development of various crops (Arango-Osorio et al., 2019).

For the S4 the possibility of reusing the effluent resulting from the liquid route was also considered, that is, the output of the P module as water for washing the facilities, a process made possible due to the efficiency of the treatment that generates an effluent with low concentrations of pollutants and biosafety. Since the pH elevation in the P module allows the inactiva- tion of pathogenic microorganisms, as discussed by Cândido et al. (2022) and Viancelli et al. (2015), than in the final effluent there was no presence of biomarkers such as Salmonella, E. coli e Porcine circovirus type 2. Matsuzaki et al. (2021) point out that Ca(OH)2 is a powerful disinfectant due to its ability to raise the alkalinity of the medium and consequently in- activate these microorganisms. Moreover, another point that corroborates this reuse practice is that presented by Bortoli et al. (2022), according to the authors the residual concentrations of NO2 and NO3 present in these effluents allow reducing the methanogenesis of waste in the gutters and promote denitrification mitigating the emissions of harmful gases to the en- vironment. It is worth mentioning that although this point was explored only for the S4, all other scenarios present the capacity for water reuse, since the inactivation principle is the increase in pH.

Still from the energy balance data (Table 1), it is possible to verify that the systems manage to supply the energy demand, having an energy surplus 3.42 times greater than the consumption in S1, 1.69 times greater than the consumption in S2, 3.13 times greater than the consumption in S3 and 1.51 times greater than the consumption in S4. The improvement of the system in these scenarios is noticeable in all the processes of the system, not only in energy aspects, the motivation of improvement of the nitrogen removal process does not justify the removal of the AD process from the liquid frac- tion since the issue of absence of carbon for denitrification can be easily solved with a by-pass. Thus, although all processes are technologically fea- sible, since in the end they can promote an efficient treatment, the scenarios with energy use of the liquid fraction should be prioritized.
48: The technical performance of the different scenarios under study high- lighted how changes in treatment system configurations affected the

individual processes in a cascading effect. In addition, all scenarios showed applicability, with minor issues that favored or worsened the results of each process. In the first stage of the systems, i.e., SLS by screening, similar values in terms of separation efficiency were adopted in all scenarios based on the values of the reference scenario (S1), 12 % efficiency for total solids (TS) removal, % for phosphorus (P) removal, 2 % for total ni- trogen (TN) removal and 8 % for total organic carbon (TOC) removal (Table 1.3a of the SM) (Fig. 2). These values are consistent with average values in the literature, such as those presented by Amaral et al. (2016), who studied the effect of SLS on biogas generation capacity for swine ma- nure, and screening efficiency that ranged from 6 % to 21 %.

However, a preliminary study (Hollas et al., 2021) found average solids removal efficiencies around 30 % for screening, emphasizing the effect of manure management, especially in terms of storage time, within the facili- ties as a critical factor in the process of solids removal via mechanical de- vices. The management inside the animal production facilities also affects the process efficiency, due to this, the volumes of sludge generated were different in the scenarios, being the ratio of 7.40E-03 Msludge Mmanure for S1 (Table 1.7a of the SM), while for S2, S3 and S4 considering an ideal man- agement, as proposed by Hollas et al. (2021), the volumetric ratio was 3.50E-02 Msludge Mmanure In addition to compromising the SLS process, the management of effluents in production facilities promotes the loss of GHG, especially methane, due to the degradation process that begins in the storage pits, reducing the potential of this waste and contributing to the GHG emissions (Im et al., 2020).

The second stage of SLS via sedimentation, the considered removal effi- ciencies (Table 1.3a of the SM) were distinct once it was identified that the management of the real scale system (S1) affected the process efficiency. Thus, for this scenario the removal efficiencies are 33 % for TS, % for total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN), 43 % for P, 7 % for the removal of TOC, besides sludge the generation compared to the initial volume about 10 While for the other scenarios (S2, S3 and S4), removal values were adopted of 60 % for TS, 2% for TAN, 80 for P, 30% for TOC. These initial stages are fundamental for functioning good operation of the subsequent units, a loss in efficiency of these processes is indicative of problems with effluent management, either within the production units, sludge manage- ment of the separation units, or in terms of overload, such as flows rates that exceed the design ones compromising the entire treatment (Vanotti et al., 2018).

In relation the Bio module, the divergences in the configurations pre- sented impacts on the systems not only in terms of biogas production, but also in its composition. The S1 presents satisfactory values in terms of bio- gas production, with 7.85 Mmanure, 76 % coming from the CLBs (0.56 Nmbiogas kgsv1), that is, the liquid fraction, and 24 % from the CSTR (0.38 Nmbiogas kgsv) that is, the solid fraction. This production results in an electrical energy generation of 1.24 kWh Nmbiogas, with an estimated heat cogeneration of 84.76 MJ Nmbiogas (Cândido et al., 2022).

The S2 have only the biogas generated from the solids system, which result in a production of 5.92 mmanure kgsv1), the value is higher than the reference scenario due to the higher efficiency of the sedimentation process that generates a sludge with a higher content of VS, in addition, the heat cogeneration is estimated at 63.97 MJ Nmbiogas-

For the S3 the liquid fraction is directed to four UASBs, reactors which leads to the production of a more energetically rich biogas, that is, with higher methane contents (around 75 %) (Zhao et al., 2021). Rico et al. (2017) highlighted that the use of UASB type reactors for the treatment of swine waste is an attractive alternative, according to the authors the combi- nation of lower hydraulic retention time (HRT), with a high organic loading rate (OLR), provides a better methanogenic activity that reflects in the rate of volumetric production of methane, resulting in a biogas with up to 80 of CH4, that is a very important point for energy aspect of utilization. Added to this, the better efficiency of the SLS, results in a biogas production of 7.34 Nmbiogas Mmanure, being, 32 % of the UASB (0.70 and 68 % of the CSTR (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsv1), and the electrical generation considered of 1.38 kWh Nmbiogas with 79.32 MJ Nmbiogas of cogenerated heat.

(2022) discussed that the process of K-struvite precipita- tion at stoichiometric doses can only be used for partial recovery of K as simulated in the present study, the authors verified the removal of about 40% of potassium, at equimolar ratios at pH values of 8 to 10, the improve- ment in removal efficiency was obtained when the system was overdosed with 100 % Mg and P and a pH of 10 resulting in 85 % recovery of K. With this, the simulated pH of 9 would possibly not be enough to favor ef- ficient K-struvite precipitation, which would demand more reagents for the process raising treatment costs. Thus, this alternative of P and K recovery, by the addition of NaOH was not configured as an effective way to jointly remove K and P from the effluent in the process under study.

S4 presents an additional step, related to the management of final sludge through composting. Although these sludges are generally stabi- lized, once they were submitted to the AD process, composting would act as a technology to reduce the humidity of these effluents, facilitating the ex- port/storage of this material (Sharara and Sadaka, 2018). At this stage, if the premises of the composting process are met, the process would occur ef- ficiently, which would result in the generation of a compost with 50 less volume and with a concentration of 0.13% of total nitrogen (TN), 0.12% of TOC, 0.12 9 of K and 0.14% of P. These values can still be im- proved, Z. Zhang et al. (2021) performed a meta-analysis and verified that the adjustment of the initial properties of the substrate, combined with control of the conditions of the composting process and even the appli- cation of additives can promote an improvement in the process and contrib- ute to the generation of a compost with better fertilizing quality. With this, there is the possibility of exploring this process as an alternative to add value to these residues (Fermoso et al., 2018).
In the other scenarios (S1, S2 and S3) the sludge generated is stored for later application to the soil (Fig. 3), which reflects another important

Fig. 3. Daily mass flow as a function of the technical performance a) TS; b) VS; c) NH3-N; d) K and f) TOC; for the four scenarios (S1, S2, S3 and S4) in relation to the solid fraction. L1-Sieve sludge; L2-Settling tank sludge; L3-CLB sludge; L4-CSTR sludge; L5-N Module sludge. L6-P Module sludge.

aspect, because with the use of these treatment systems the management of this material is facilitated, the scenarios S1 and S3 end up having a lower sludge generation, the required agricultural area (3.77 m² m for S1 and 8.68 m² m for S3) to support the nutrient load (estimated as a function of P concentration as presented in the SM) is lower when compared to sce- narios S2 and S4 (12 m² m and 11.5 m² m respectively), it is worth noting that the CRST sludge is rich in nutrients due to the previous SLS step, concentrating the highest contents of P and K, which is favorable for the development of various crops (Arango-Osorio et al., 2019).

For the S4 the possibility of reusing the effluent resulting from the liquid route was also considered, that is, the output of the P module as water for washing the facilities, a process made possible due to the efficiency of the treatment that generates an effluent with low concentrations of pollutants and biosafety. Since the pH elevation in the P module allows the inactiva- tion of pathogenic microorganisms, as discussed by Cândido et al. (2022) and Viancelli et al. (2015), than in the final effluent there was no presence of biomarkers such as Salmonella, E. coli e Porcine circovirus type 2. Matsuzaki et al. (2021) point out that Ca(OH)2 is a powerful disinfectant due to its ability to raise the alkalinity of the medium and consequently in- activate these microorganisms. Moreover, another point that corroborates this reuse practice is that presented by Bortoli et al. (2022), according to the authors the residual concentrations of NO2 and NO3 present in these effluents allow reducing the methanogenesis of waste in the gutters and promote denitrification mitigating the emissions of harmful gases to the en- vironment. It is worth mentioning that although this point was explored only for the S4, all other scenarios present the capacity for water reuse, since the inactivation principle is the increase in pH.

Still from the energy balance data (Table 1), it is possible to verify that the systems manage to supply the energy demand, having an energy surplus 3.42 times greater than the consumption in S1, 1.69 times greater than the consumption in S2, 3.13 times greater than the consumption in S3 and 1.51 times greater than the consumption in S4. The improvement of the system in these scenarios is noticeable in all the processes of the system, not only in energy aspects, the motivation of improvement of the nitrogen removal process does not justify the removal of the AD process from the liquid frac- tion since the issue of absence of carbon for denitrification can be easily solved with a by-pass. Thus, although all processes are technologically fea- sible, since in the end they can promote an efficient treatment, the scenarios with energy use of the liquid fraction should be prioritized.
49: The technical performance of the different scenarios under study high- lighted how changes in treatment system configurations affected the

individual processes in a cascading effect. In addition, all scenarios showed applicability, with minor issues that favored or worsened the results of each process. In the first stage of the systems, i.e., SLS by screening, similar values in terms of separation efficiency were adopted in all scenarios based on the values of the reference scenario (S1), 12 % efficiency for total solids (TS) removal, % for phosphorus (P) removal, 2 % for total ni- trogen (TN) removal and 8 % for total organic carbon (TOC) removal (Table 1.3a of the SM) (Fig. 2). These values are consistent with average values in the literature, such as those presented by Amaral et al. (2016), who studied the effect of SLS on biogas generation capacity for swine ma- nure, and screening efficiency that ranged from 6 % to 21 %.

However, a preliminary study (Hollas et al., 2021) found average solids removal efficiencies around 30 % for screening, emphasizing the effect of manure management, especially in terms of storage time, within the facili- ties as a critical factor in the process of solids removal via mechanical de- vices. The management inside the animal production facilities also affects the process efficiency, due to this, the volumes of sludge generated were different in the scenarios, being the ratio of 7.40E-03 Msludge Mmanure for S1 (Table 1.7a of the SM), while for S2, S3 and S4 considering an ideal man- agement, as proposed by Hollas et al. (2021), the volumetric ratio was 3.50E-02 Msludge Mmanure In addition to compromising the SLS process, the management of effluents in production facilities promotes the loss of GHG, especially methane, due to the degradation process that begins in the storage pits, reducing the potential of this waste and contributing to the GHG emissions (Im et al., 2020).

The second stage of SLS via sedimentation, the considered removal effi- ciencies (Table 1.3a of the SM) were distinct once it was identified that the management of the real scale system (S1) affected the process efficiency. Thus, for this scenario the removal efficiencies are 33 % for TS, % for total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN), 43 % for P, 7 % for the removal of TOC, besides sludge the generation compared to the initial volume about 10 While for the other scenarios (S2, S3 and S4), removal values were adopted of 60 % for TS, 2% for TAN, 80 for P, 30% for TOC. These initial stages are fundamental for functioning good operation of the subsequent units, a loss in efficiency of these processes is indicative of problems with effluent management, either within the production units, sludge manage- ment of the separation units, or in terms of overload, such as flows rates that exceed the design ones compromising the entire treatment (Vanotti et al., 2018).

In relation the Bio module, the divergences in the configurations pre- sented impacts on the systems not only in terms of biogas production, but also in its composition. The S1 presents satisfactory values in terms of bio- gas production, with 7.85 Mmanure, 76 % coming from the CLBs (0.56 Nmbiogas kgsv1), that is, the liquid fraction, and 24 % from the CSTR (0.38 Nmbiogas kgsv) that is, the solid fraction. This production results in an electrical energy generation of 1.24 kWh Nmbiogas, with an estimated heat cogeneration of 84.76 MJ Nmbiogas (Cândido et al., 2022).

The S2 have only the biogas generated from the solids system, which result in a production of 5.92 mmanure kgsv1), the value is higher than the reference scenario due to the higher efficiency of the sedimentation process that generates a sludge with a higher content of VS, in addition, the heat cogeneration is estimated at 63.97 MJ Nmbiogas-

For the S3 the liquid fraction is directed to four UASBs, reactors which leads to the production of a more energetically rich biogas, that is, with higher methane contents (around 75 %) (Zhao et al., 2021). Rico et al. (2017) highlighted that the use of UASB type reactors for the treatment of swine waste is an attractive alternative, according to the authors the combi- nation of lower hydraulic retention time (HRT), with a high organic loading rate (OLR), provides a better methanogenic activity that reflects in the rate of volumetric production of methane, resulting in a biogas with up to 80 of CH4, that is a very important point for energy aspect of utilization. Added to this, the better efficiency of the SLS, results in a biogas production of 7.34 Nmbiogas Mmanure, being, 32 % of the UASB (0.70 and 68 % of the CSTR (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsv1), and the electrical generation considered of 1.38 kWh Nmbiogas with 79.32 MJ Nmbiogas of cogenerated heat.

In S4 only the sludge is used energetically by means of AD, but in this scenario the sludge resulting from N module is not directed to the CSTR re- actor reducing by half the input flow rate, but practically doubling the VS concentration, with this the biogas production was estimated at 5.30 Mmanure (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsu1 already the heat cogeneration was 57.28 MJ Nmbiogas

The improvement in the flow of solids directly affects the strategy of en- ergy use, since the scenarios with the absence of the reactor for the use of the liquid fraction (S2 and S4), do not have such significant losses com- pared to the other scenarios due to the better existing management. Favor- ing the digestion of solids through the CSTR reactor is important to the system not only to ensure the life of the CLB type reactors (present in S1) or UASB type (present in S3), but also because of the reduction of the oscil- lations of gas production due to the year seasonality effect, the cold months tend to reduce the gas production in reactors that do not have a heating system (Yan et al., 2022). Schmidt et al. (2019) found that the biogas pro- duction was affected by 30 % with the reduction of 7 °C in the system, compromising the stability of the process and the methane production. Thus, promoting efficient separation and the use of sludge reactors ensures that the solids will have a degradation with less interference all year round, maximized the biogas productivity of the plant, reducing the need for more reactor units.

After the AD, the effluent is directed to the N module. All scenarios pres- ent operational potentials with the conditions imposed at the module inlet. For this process one of the main factors that impact the different scenarios is the bioavailability of organic carbon at the system influent (Hollas et al., 2019). Since in the absence or insufficient supply of organic matter, the de- nitrification process occurs incompletely and can cause damage in terms of reducing the buffering potential, reducing the system pH and compromising the removal efficiency (Lee et al., 2019; Shi et al., 2020). Besides being potentially dangerous to the environment due to the higher probability of nitrous oxide (N20) losses from incomplete denitrification, since N2O is one of the intermediates of the process (Bortoli et al., 2022; Dai et al., 2021).

In the scenarios where the previous step was AD (S1 and S3), the carbon input is limited, since it was previously consumed by the AD process, thus in hot months where the efficiency of the Bio module is above average, there is a risk of the N removal system collapsing if organic matter supplementa- tion is not done (Wang et al., 2018). With the reduction in denitrification and the consequent decrease in pH, the presence of nitrite must be carefully monitored SO that inhibition by nitrous acid does not affect the system (Jiang et al., 2019). However this can be easily solved by diverting a frac- tion of the Bio module's input flow, ensuring the operation of the N module, as demonstrated by Awad et al. (2022), who found that increasing the carbon supply in the anoxic zone by means of a by-pass improved TN removal efficiency by about %.

For S2 and S4, the high availability of carbon guarantees the efficient functioning of N module, however the increase of dissolved organic carbon and the fraction of solids in the effluent that enters the system causes another problem, related to sludge generation (Table 1.5a of the SM). The higher carbon bioavailability favors the growth of heterotrophic organisms, which translates into a high generation of sludge, besides increasing the oxygen demand of the system, either through the difficulty in mass transfer, due to the higher content of solids, or by the demand for oxidation of these organic compounds (Steuernagel et al., 2018). Consequently the energy ex- penditure of the system is high (Table 1), being considered about 2 times higher than the reference scenario. Wang et al. (2021) reported that nitrifi- cation was severely compromised by the excess of organic compounds, also affecting the flocculation and sedimentation capacity of the nitrifying bio- mass, which adds to the generated sludge management problem (W. M. et al., 2020).

The high generation of sludge in the N module can be considered a problem beyond the energy aspect, since this waste does not present high fertilizing power (Fig. 3), nor even high potential for biogas generation (75 + 8 mLNCH4 gvs ). In S2, where the surplus sludge from N module is directed to the CSTR, this sludge ends up diluting the solids concentration up to 49 %, without having large contributions in biogas generation,

increasing the heating system demand about 1.7 times compared to the other scenarios. The high volume of sludge presents a high cost of treat- ment, according to Domini et al. (2022) for the year 2021, the average price of sludge treatment in Northern Italy averaged 176.9 ton consid- ering loading, transport, recovery/disposal and analysis costs. Not promot- ing the generation of excess sludge in the systems should be a priority since it has techno-economic consequences in the treatment plants. Thus, the elimination of the AD systems predecessors to this module can bring losses to the process operation.

P module the effluent characteristics resulted from N module will directly reflect on the performance of the P precipitation process (Table 1.6a of the SM). In S1 the Ca(OH)2 demand is about 4.5 times lower than for S2, because the higher alkalinity present in the effluent resulting from N module in S2, has a higher buffer capacity requiring more Ca(OH)2 for pH adjustment to the ideal precipitation conditions. Also the more and impure sludge is generated due to the remained TOC concentration that is coprecipitated. Christensen et al. (2022) studied the effects of organic matter on P precipita- tion and found a relatively large difference in the amount of precipitate (>50% for the liquid sample with a low carbon content compared to the other samples with a higher organic matter content, which also reflected in a precipitate with 20 % organic materials in the composition.

In S3 and S4 the precipitation of K-struvite was simulated to promote the removal of K, since this nutrient has no effective removal in any of the treatment modules. Theoretically equimolar ratios of K:P:Mg would be sufficient to promote K-struvite precipitation, so as this assumption was met in all scenarios, this process was simulated with the addition of NaOH in the effluent up to pH 9 (Le et al., 2021). With this, the NaOH de- mand would be 0.17 kg for S3 and 0.50 for S4, according to the test performed (described in 5.A in the SM). This higher demand in S4 is due to the same reason as S2, due to the higher alkalinity of the efflu- ent and consequent buffering of the medium. Although theoretically the ratios of K:P:Mg meet the premises of the process, the presence of Ca and residual NH3-N in the effluent, possibly would not promote the effective precipitation of K-struvite, but the precipitation of calcium phosphate or struvite, not removing K as expected. Furthermore, the ratio of K is about 10 times higher than the ratios of Mg and P, thus, supplementation of P and Mg in the effluent would be necessary to promote K removal.

Kabdasli et al. (2022) discussed that the process of K-struvite precipita- tion at stoichiometric doses can only be used for partial recovery of K as simulated in the present study, the authors verified the removal of about 40% of potassium, at equimolar ratios at pH values of 8 to 10, the improve- ment in removal efficiency was obtained when the system was overdosed with 100 % Mg and P and a pH of 10 resulting in 85 % recovery of K. With this, the simulated pH of 9 would possibly not be enough to favor ef- ficient K-struvite precipitation, which would demand more reagents for the process raising treatment costs. Thus, this alternative of P and K recovery, by the addition of NaOH was not configured as an effective way to jointly remove K and P from the effluent in the process under study.

S4 presents an additional step, related to the management of final sludge through composting. Although these sludges are generally stabi- lized, once they were submitted to the AD process, composting would act as a technology to reduce the humidity of these effluents, facilitating the ex- port/storage of this material (Sharara and Sadaka, 2018). At this stage, if the premises of the composting process are met, the process would occur ef- ficiently, which would result in the generation of a compost with 50 less volume and with a concentration of 0.13% of total nitrogen (TN), 0.12% of TOC, 0.12 9 of K and 0.14% of P. These values can still be im- proved, Z. Zhang et al. (2021) performed a meta-analysis and verified that the adjustment of the initial properties of the substrate, combined with control of the conditions of the composting process and even the appli- cation of additives can promote an improvement in the process and contrib- ute to the generation of a compost with better fertilizing quality. With this, there is the possibility of exploring this process as an alternative to add value to these residues (Fermoso et al., 2018).
In the other scenarios (S1, S2 and S3) the sludge generated is stored for later application to the soil (Fig. 3), which reflects another important

Technical performance for TS (a), VS (b), TAN (c), P (d), K (e) and TOC (f) for the four scenarios (S1, S2, S3 and S4) along the proposed treatment systems compared (P1 to P6) in relation to the liquid fraction. (Were C/C = Characteristics of raw manure and effluent at the end of each module. C = TSo: 16380 mg L-1; VS0: 11440 mg L-1; NH3-No: 1293.6 mg L-1 P0: 462.64 mg L-1; K0: 757.35 mg L-1; TOC: 3585.37 mg L-1.

In S4 only the sludge is used energetically by means of AD, but in this scenario the sludge resulting from N module is not directed to the CSTR re- actor reducing by half the input flow rate, but practically doubling the VS concentration, with this the biogas production was estimated at 5.30 Mmanure (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsu1 already the heat cogeneration was 57.28 MJ Nmbiogas

The improvement in the flow of solids directly affects the strategy of en- ergy use, since the scenarios with the absence of the reactor for the use of the liquid fraction (S2 and S4), do not have such significant losses com- pared to the other scenarios due to the better existing management. Favor- ing the digestion of solids through the CSTR reactor is important to the system not only to ensure the life of the CLB type reactors (present in S1) or UASB type (present in S3), but also because of the reduction of the oscil- lations of gas production due to the year seasonality effect, the cold months tend to reduce the gas production in reactors that do not have a heating system (Yan et al., 2022). Schmidt et al. (2019) found that the biogas pro- duction was affected by 30 % with the reduction of 7 °C in the system, compromising the stability of the process and the methane production. Thus, promoting efficient separation and the use of sludge reactors ensures that the solids will have a degradation with less interference all year round, maximized the biogas productivity of the plant, reducing the need for more reactor units.

After the AD, the effluent is directed to the N module. All scenarios pres- ent operational potentials with the conditions imposed at the module inlet. For this process one of the main factors that impact the different scenarios is the bioavailability of organic carbon at the system influent (Hollas et al., 2019). Since in the absence or insufficient supply of organic matter, the de- nitrification process occurs incompletely and can cause damage in terms of reducing the buffering potential, reducing the system pH and compromising the removal efficiency (Lee et al., 2019; Shi et al., 2020). Besides being potentially dangerous to the environment due to the higher probability of nitrous oxide (N20) losses from incomplete denitrification, since N2O is one of the intermediates of the process (Bortoli et al., 2022; Dai et al., 2021).

In the scenarios where the previous step was AD (S1 and S3), the carbon input is limited, since it was previously consumed by the AD process, thus in hot months where the efficiency of the Bio module is above average, there is a risk of the N removal system collapsing if organic matter supplementa- tion is not done (Wang et al., 2018). With the reduction in denitrification and the consequent decrease in pH, the presence of nitrite must be carefully monitored SO that inhibition by nitrous acid does not affect the system (Jiang et al., 2019). However this can be easily solved by diverting a frac- tion of the Bio module's input flow, ensuring the operation of the N module, as demonstrated by Awad et al. (2022), who found that increasing the carbon supply in the anoxic zone by means of a by-pass improved TN removal efficiency by about %.

For S2 and S4, the high availability of carbon guarantees the efficient functioning of N module, however the increase of dissolved organic carbon and the fraction of solids in the effluent that enters the system causes another problem, related to sludge generation (Table 1.5a of the SM). The higher carbon bioavailability favors the growth of heterotrophic organisms, which translates into a high generation of sludge, besides increasing the oxygen demand of the system, either through the difficulty in mass transfer, due to the higher content of solids, or by the demand for oxidation of these organic compounds (Steuernagel et al., 2018). Consequently the energy ex- penditure of the system is high (Table 1), being considered about 2 times higher than the reference scenario. Wang et al. (2021) reported that nitrifi- cation was severely compromised by the excess of organic compounds, also affecting the flocculation and sedimentation capacity of the nitrifying bio- mass, which adds to the generated sludge management problem (W. M. et al., 2020).

The high generation of sludge in the N module can be considered a problem beyond the energy aspect, since this waste does not present high fertilizing power (Fig. 3), nor even high potential for biogas generation (75 + 8 mLNCH4 gvs ). In S2, where the surplus sludge from N module is directed to the CSTR, this sludge ends up diluting the solids concentration up to 49 %, without having large contributions in biogas generation,

increasing the heating system demand about 1.7 times compared to the other scenarios. The high volume of sludge presents a high cost of treat- ment, according to Domini et al. (2022) for the year 2021, the average price of sludge treatment in Northern Italy averaged 176.9 ton consid- ering loading, transport, recovery/disposal and analysis costs. Not promot- ing the generation of excess sludge in the systems should be a priority since it has techno-economic consequences in the treatment plants. Thus, the elimination of the AD systems predecessors to this module can bring losses to the process operation.

P module the effluent characteristics resulted from N module will directly reflect on the performance of the P precipitation process (Table 1.6a of the SM). In S1 the Ca(OH)2 demand is about 4.5 times lower than for S2, because the higher alkalinity present in the effluent resulting from N module in S2, has a higher buffer capacity requiring more Ca(OH)2 for pH adjustment to the ideal precipitation conditions. Also the more and impure sludge is generated due to the remained TOC concentration that is coprecipitated. Christensen et al. (2022) studied the effects of organic matter on P precipita- tion and found a relatively large difference in the amount of precipitate (>50% for the liquid sample with a low carbon content compared to the other samples with a higher organic matter content, which also reflected in a precipitate with 20 % organic materials in the composition.

In S3 and S4 the precipitation of K-struvite was simulated to promote the removal of K, since this nutrient has no effective removal in any of the treatment modules. Theoretically equimolar ratios of K:P:Mg would be sufficient to promote K-struvite precipitation, so as this assumption was met in all scenarios, this process was simulated with the addition of NaOH in the effluent up to pH 9 (Le et al., 2021). With this, the NaOH de- mand would be 0.17 kg for S3 and 0.50 for S4, according to the test performed (described in 5.A in the SM). This higher demand in S4 is due to the same reason as S2, due to the higher alkalinity of the efflu- ent and consequent buffering of the medium. Although theoretically the ratios of K:P:Mg meet the premises of the process, the presence of Ca and residual NH3-N in the effluent, possibly would not promote the effective precipitation of K-struvite, but the precipitation of calcium phosphate or struvite, not removing K as expected. Furthermore, the ratio of K is about 10 times higher than the ratios of Mg and P, thus, supplementation of P and Mg in the effluent would be necessary to promote K removal.

Kabdasli et al. (2022) discussed that the process of K-struvite precipita- tion at stoichiometric doses can only be used for partial recovery of K as simulated in the present study, the authors verified the removal of about 40% of potassium, at equimolar ratios at pH values of 8 to 10, the improve- ment in removal efficiency was obtained when the system was overdosed with 100 % Mg and P and a pH of 10 resulting in 85 % recovery of K. With this, the simulated pH of 9 would possibly not be enough to favor ef- ficient K-struvite precipitation, which would demand more reagents for the process raising treatment costs. Thus, this alternative of P and K recovery, by the addition of NaOH was not configured as an effective way to jointly remove K and P from the effluent in the process under study.

S4 presents an additional step, related to the management of final sludge through composting. Although these sludges are generally stabi- lized, once they were submitted to the AD process, composting would act as a technology to reduce the humidity of these effluents, facilitating the ex- port/storage of this material (Sharara and Sadaka, 2018). At this stage, if the premises of the composting process are met, the process would occur ef- ficiently, which would result in the generation of a compost with 50 less volume and with a concentration of 0.13% of total nitrogen (TN), 0.12% of TOC, 0.12 9 of K and 0.14% of P. These values can still be im- proved, Z. Zhang et al. (2021) performed a meta-analysis and verified that the adjustment of the initial properties of the substrate, combined with control of the conditions of the composting process and even the appli- cation of additives can promote an improvement in the process and contrib- ute to the generation of a compost with better fertilizing quality. With this, there is the possibility of exploring this process as an alternative to add value to these residues (Fermoso et al., 2018).
In the other scenarios (S1, S2 and S3) the sludge generated is stored for later application to the soil (Fig. 3), which reflects another important

Fig. 3. Daily mass flow as a function of the technical performance a) TS; b) VS; c) NH3-N; d) K and f) TOC; for the four scenarios (S1, S2, S3 and S4) in relation to the solid fraction. L1-Sieve sludge; L2-Settling tank sludge; L3-CLB sludge; L4-CSTR sludge; L5-N Module sludge. L6-P Module sludge.

aspect, because with the use of these treatment systems the management of this material is facilitated, the scenarios S1 and S3 end up having a lower sludge generation, the required agricultural area (3.77 m² m for S1 and 8.68 m² m for S3) to support the nutrient load (estimated as a function of P concentration as presented in the SM) is lower when compared to sce- narios S2 and S4 (12 m² m and 11.5 m² m respectively), it is worth noting that the CRST sludge is rich in nutrients due to the previous SLS step, concentrating the highest contents of P and K, which is favorable for the development of various crops (Arango-Osorio et al., 2019).

For the S4 the possibility of reusing the effluent resulting from the liquid route was also considered, that is, the output of the P module as water for washing the facilities, a process made possible due to the efficiency of the treatment that generates an effluent with low concentrations of pollutants and biosafety. Since the pH elevation in the P module allows the inactiva- tion of pathogenic microorganisms, as discussed by Cândido et al. (2022) and Viancelli et al. (2015), than in the final effluent there was no presence of biomarkers such as Salmonella, E. coli e Porcine circovirus type 2. Matsuzaki et al. (2021) point out that Ca(OH)2 is a powerful disinfectant due to its ability to raise the alkalinity of the medium and consequently in- activate these microorganisms. Moreover, another point that corroborates this reuse practice is that presented by Bortoli et al. (2022), according to the authors the residual concentrations of NO2 and NO3 present in these effluents allow reducing the methanogenesis of waste in the gutters and promote denitrification mitigating the emissions of harmful gases to the en- vironment. It is worth mentioning that although this point was explored only for the S4, all other scenarios present the capacity for water reuse, since the inactivation principle is the increase in pH.

Still from the energy balance data (Table 1), it is possible to verify that the systems manage to supply the energy demand, having an energy surplus 3.42 times greater than the consumption in S1, 1.69 times greater than the consumption in S2, 3.13 times greater than the consumption in S3 and 1.51 times greater than the consumption in S4. The improvement of the system in these scenarios is noticeable in all the processes of the system, not only in energy aspects, the motivation of improvement of the nitrogen removal process does not justify the removal of the AD process from the liquid frac- tion since the issue of absence of carbon for denitrification can be easily solved with a by-pass. Thus, although all processes are technologically fea- sible, since in the end they can promote an efficient treatment, the scenarios with energy use of the liquid fraction should be prioritized.
51: The technical performance of the different scenarios under study high- lighted how changes in treatment system configurations affected the

individual processes in a cascading effect. In addition, all scenarios showed applicability, with minor issues that favored or worsened the results of each process. In the first stage of the systems, i.e., SLS by screening, similar values in terms of separation efficiency were adopted in all scenarios based on the values of the reference scenario (S1), 12 % efficiency for total solids (TS) removal, % for phosphorus (P) removal, 2 % for total ni- trogen (TN) removal and 8 % for total organic carbon (TOC) removal (Table 1.3a of the SM) (Fig. 2). These values are consistent with average values in the literature, such as those presented by Amaral et al. (2016), who studied the effect of SLS on biogas generation capacity for swine ma- nure, and screening efficiency that ranged from 6 % to 21 %.

However, a preliminary study (Hollas et al., 2021) found average solids removal efficiencies around 30 % for screening, emphasizing the effect of manure management, especially in terms of storage time, within the facili- ties as a critical factor in the process of solids removal via mechanical de- vices. The management inside the animal production facilities also affects the process efficiency, due to this, the volumes of sludge generated were different in the scenarios, being the ratio of 7.40E-03 Msludge Mmanure for S1 (Table 1.7a of the SM), while for S2, S3 and S4 considering an ideal man- agement, as proposed by Hollas et al. (2021), the volumetric ratio was 3.50E-02 Msludge Mmanure In addition to compromising the SLS process, the management of effluents in production facilities promotes the loss of GHG, especially methane, due to the degradation process that begins in the storage pits, reducing the potential of this waste and contributing to the GHG emissions (Im et al., 2020).

The second stage of SLS via sedimentation, the considered removal effi- ciencies (Table 1.3a of the SM) were distinct once it was identified that the management of the real scale system (S1) affected the process efficiency. Thus, for this scenario the removal efficiencies are 33 % for TS, % for total ammoniacal nitrogen (TAN), 43 % for P, 7 % for the removal of TOC, besides sludge the generation compared to the initial volume about 10 While for the other scenarios (S2, S3 and S4), removal values were adopted of 60 % for TS, 2% for TAN, 80 for P, 30% for TOC. These initial stages are fundamental for functioning good operation of the subsequent units, a loss in efficiency of these processes is indicative of problems with effluent management, either within the production units, sludge manage- ment of the separation units, or in terms of overload, such as flows rates that exceed the design ones compromising the entire treatment (Vanotti et al., 2018).

In relation the Bio module, the divergences in the configurations pre- sented impacts on the systems not only in terms of biogas production, but also in its composition. The S1 presents satisfactory values in terms of bio- gas production, with 7.85 Mmanure, 76 % coming from the CLBs (0.56 Nmbiogas kgsv1), that is, the liquid fraction, and 24 % from the CSTR (0.38 Nmbiogas kgsv) that is, the solid fraction. This production results in an electrical energy generation of 1.24 kWh Nmbiogas, with an estimated heat cogeneration of 84.76 MJ Nmbiogas (Cândido et al., 2022).

The S2 have only the biogas generated from the solids system, which result in a production of 5.92 mmanure kgsv1), the value is higher than the reference scenario due to the higher efficiency of the sedimentation process that generates a sludge with a higher content of VS, in addition, the heat cogeneration is estimated at 63.97 MJ Nmbiogas-

For the S3 the liquid fraction is directed to four UASBs, reactors which leads to the production of a more energetically rich biogas, that is, with higher methane contents (around 75 %) (Zhao et al., 2021). Rico et al. (2017) highlighted that the use of UASB type reactors for the treatment of swine waste is an attractive alternative, according to the authors the combi- nation of lower hydraulic retention time (HRT), with a high organic loading rate (OLR), provides a better methanogenic activity that reflects in the rate of volumetric production of methane, resulting in a biogas with up to 80 of CH4, that is a very important point for energy aspect of utilization. Added to this, the better efficiency of the SLS, results in a biogas production of 7.34 Nmbiogas Mmanure, being, 32 % of the UASB (0.70 and 68 % of the CSTR (0.37 Nmbiogas kgsv1), and the electrical generation considered of 1.38 kWh Nmbiogas with 79.32 MJ Nmbiogas of cogenerated heat.

At this stage, if the premises of the composting process are met, the process would occur ef- ficiently, which would result in the generation of a compost with 50 less volume and with a concentration of 0.13% of total nitrogen (TN), 0.12% of TOC, 0.12 9 of K and 0.14% of P.
