Jeong et al. (2018) observed that composting of manure increased the GW potential by 35 compared to the untreated scenarios, however the use of compost for fertilizing rice paddies had a ben- eficial impact on reducing CH4 losses, due to the generation of a stable com- post with lower volume but high fertilizing power. In the present study, the energy matrix is renewable based on hydraulic sources, which ends up the most significant impacts tending towards the stage that makes use of fossil sources, in this case the use of fuels for soil application (Goulart Coelho and Lange, 2018; Li et al., 2021).

The ammonia losses by volatilization in the composting stage of S4, make it present the worst result regarding TA, about 88 % higher than SB. In the other scenarios this emission is more significant in the N module, for S2 (8.24E-01 SO2 eq) and S3 (2.64E-01 SO2 due to the higher pH, which contributes to NH3-N losses; besides the application to the soil, which corresponds to 2.08E-01 kg SO2 eq in the S1, 2.95E-01 kg SO2 in the S2, 2.99E-01 kg SO2 eq in the S3 and 1.34 kg SO2 eq in the SB. In the SOD category, the SB also presents an impact on average 85 + 4% higher than the other scenarios evaluated, with of the impact attributed to the fertilization step, the most significant step also for the other scenarios, contributing on average 65 + of the impact. In this category the losses of N2O and NO caused by fertilization are the main contributors to the adverse burden, in addition, the losses in the composting stage of scenario S4 are responsible for the emission of 6.85E-04 kg CFC11 eq

The fertilization stage is one of the largest contributors to the environ- mental burden of GW potential in all scenarios, as well as to the categories of SOD and TA, due to the losses promoted both by the sprinkling applica- tion process, which promotes the loss of nutrients and gases, and the use of fossil fuels, which promote the release of heavy metals, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and other toxic compounds into the environment (Ioannou- Ttofa et al., 2021). Duan et al. (2020) found a positive correlation between the distance from the farms and the biogas plants, the reducing the trans- port route by 5 km promoted a reduction of 118° of damage in GW poten- tial, while an increase in the distance traveled to 100 km increases damage by 629 %, which corroborates the data of the present study, since the volume of sludge and consequent increase in the area of application was the main divergence between the scenarios.

The impacts of the OFTE category are mainly due to emissions from the CHP plant (for S1, S2, S3 and S4) and the composting module in the S4, while in SB fertilization is the most significant damage step (98 %), with the NOx and methane losses involved in these steps being the main environ- mental burdens (Hollas et al., 2022). In this category, S1, S2, S3 and S4 present negative values, i.e., the products generated promote environmen- tal benefits capable of mitigating the impact generated. In general, the avoided use of nitrogen and phosphorus, and the use of heat and conse- quent avoided use of wood, were the most significant steps that justify these favorable values in the scenarios. The use of the resources available in the waste, especially in terms of substitutes for commercial fertilizers, presents a benefit in all categories, the reduction of impacts related to the production and transport of synthetic fertilizers, reflects directly on the life cycle of these systems. Thus, the recovery of these resources in a context of depletion of natural sources, such as P, is essential for the promotion of food security combined with the reduction of environmental damage (Walling and Vaneeckhaute, 2020).

The FE and TE categories are the only ones in which the SB is configured as having one of the lowest impacts in relation to the other scenarios. This occurs in terms of FE, due to the emission of the P module in relation to the discharge of effluent into water bodies, which still presents a load of P and

N contributing to water contamination in this aspect, in addition to the de- mand for chemical additives to promote the precipitation of P. As in S4 the effluent is reused for swine rearing facilities, there is no such discharge and therefore the results of this configuration are the best. The possibility of having water for reuse at the end of the process is extremely important in a context of water scarcity. Ensuring this resource, especially considering the high water consumption that pig farming presents, is essential for the viability of this activity (Ijoma et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2020). Thus, as the other scenarios also have the capacity to recover the resource, their damage can be reduced if this practice is implemented. Besides the benefit of water reuse, the use of solid fertilizers promotes a better use of the resources with less losses to the environment, due to the slow release of nutrients, unlike liquid biofertilizers that end up having greater losses by leaching, contribut- ing to improve the performance of this scenario (Corbala-Robles et al., 2018; Jeong et al., 2018).

For TE, in S4 the benefits promoted by resource recovery are not able to reduce the damages, due to the losses in the process, the composting is re- sponsible for about 70 % of the impacts, making this system present the worst performance. In the other systems the impacts are consistent with the findings of Ugwu et al. (2022) and Ramírez-Arpide et al. (2018) where the emission of CHP was one of the critical steps in this category. However, it is possible to verify negative values for TE in the other scenarios (SB, S1, S2 and S3), that is, they present the complete mitigation of the damages generated, this due to the recoveries of NPK fertilizers, which mit- igate on average 11 + 12% of the damages. In addition, heat and power utilization contribute on average for 20 + 20.5 of the benefits of the systems in scenarios S1, S2, S3 and S4.

Due to the large influence of the fertilization step, a sensitivity analysis was performed to verify the effects of different emission factors on the performance of the impact categories evaluated. In general, the highest sen- sitivities were seen for the categories Global warming, Stratospheric ozone depletion and Terrestrial acidification, for scenarios S1, S2 and S3 (Fig. 2.7a in supplementary material). While for S4, in addition to these the category Freshwater eutrophication was also one of the categories that showed the greatest variations.

For Global warming the 40 increase or decrease in emission rates from fertilizer application promoted changes of 25 + 2% on average in GHG emis- sions for S1, S2 and S3, and 37 + % for 60% change in emission rates from fertilizer application. While for S4 the change was around % (to + %) and % for increase or decrease in emission rates from fertilizer appli- cation. For Terrestrial acidification, scenarios S1 and S3 were the most sensi- tive with changes averaging 46 + 6% (to + 40%). In the Stratospheric ozone depletion category, scenarios S2 and S3 presented a change around considering an increase or decrease of 60 in fertilization emission rates.

In S4 Freshwater eutrophication ranged from 13 to 0%, with increased or decreased emissions of 40 and 60%. These results corroborate those ob- served by Duan et al. (2020), who verified in sensitivity studies of emissions from the application of digestate, that emissions from fertilization should not be underestimated, and therefore the adoption of good practices is fun- damental to make these technological routes viable.

The improvement of the processes highlights the importance of waste management for the minimization of environmental damage. The choice of technologies and treatment routes has a direct impact on the damage to the environment, as can be seen in the present results. Furthermore, the advantages of using energy from the swine waste are evident with the reduction of impacts in all categories compared to the scenario without using energy from the waste, AD plays a key role in this sense, since it cap- tures the noxious gases and enables energy recovery from them (Fermoso et al., 2018). Thus, corroborating the technical data, the routes that priori- tized the AD, present in S1 and S3, presented the best environmental perfor- mances (Yong et al., 2021).
59: Table 4 presents the environmental performance of the scenarios under study as well as an additional scenario (SB) to better understand the signif- icance of adopting treatment technologies. Analyzing the results by impact category, for GW of the steps involved in SB, it is possible to verify that the step that contributes most significantly to the GW potential are the fertiliza- tion processes, emitting 147 kg of CO2 per m³ of waste and representing 60 % of the total value of this category, in addition to the step of storing the waste for 80 days, which presents 35 % of the impacts of this category for this scenario, as illustrated in Fig. 4. Kuhn et al. (2018) discuss that CH4 emissions from storage and direct N2O emissions after manure applica- tion are the main causes of GW potential, like the results observed in the present study.

In the other scenarios S2, S3 and S4 the same behavior for the GW cat- egory is observed, i.e. the fertilization step and the N module emissions are the most significant steps for the category. Despite these losses, adoption of treatment systems (S1, S2, S3 and S4) promoted a mitigation of 86 + 3% of GW potential compared to the baseline scenario SB. Also, for scenario S2 the emissions from the storage pond (7.96 CO2 also contribute signif- icantly to the final impact and in S4 the composting step contributes of the impacts in the category. Jeong et al. (2018) observed that composting of manure increased the GW potential by 35 compared to the untreated scenarios, however the use of compost for fertilizing rice paddies had a ben- eficial impact on reducing CH4 losses, due to the generation of a stable com- post with lower volume but high fertilizing power. In the present study, the energy matrix is renewable based on hydraulic sources, which ends up the most significant impacts tending towards the stage that makes use of fossil sources, in this case the use of fuels for soil application (Goulart Coelho and Lange, 2018; Li et al., 2021).

The ammonia losses by volatilization in the composting stage of S4, make it present the worst result regarding TA, about 88 % higher than SB. In the other scenarios this emission is more significant in the N module, for S2 (8.24E-01 SO2 eq) and S3 (2.64E-01 SO2 due to the higher pH, which contributes to NH3-N losses; besides the application to the soil, which corresponds to 2.08E-01 kg SO2 eq in the S1, 2.95E-01 kg SO2 in the S2, 2.99E-01 kg SO2 eq in the S3 and 1.34 kg SO2 eq in the SB. In the SOD category, the SB also presents an impact on average 85 + 4% higher than the other scenarios evaluated, with of the impact attributed to the fertilization step, the most significant step also for the other scenarios, contributing on average 65 + of the impact. In this category the losses of N2O and NO caused by fertilization are the main contributors to the adverse burden, in addition, the losses in the composting stage of scenario S4 are responsible for the emission of 6.85E-04 kg CFC11 eq

The fertilization stage is one of the largest contributors to the environ- mental burden of GW potential in all scenarios, as well as to the categories of SOD and TA, due to the losses promoted both by the sprinkling applica- tion process, which promotes the loss of nutrients and gases, and the use of fossil fuels, which promote the release of heavy metals, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and other toxic compounds into the environment (Ioannou- Ttofa et al., 2021). Duan et al. (2020) found a positive correlation between the distance from the farms and the biogas plants, the reducing the trans- port route by 5 km promoted a reduction of 118° of damage in GW poten- tial, while an increase in the distance traveled to 100 km increases damage by 629 %, which corroborates the data of the present study, since the volume of sludge and consequent increase in the area of application was the main divergence between the scenarios.

The impacts of the OFTE category are mainly due to emissions from the CHP plant (for S1, S2, S3 and S4) and the composting module in the S4, while in SB fertilization is the most significant damage step (98 %), with the NOx and methane losses involved in these steps being the main environ- mental burdens (Hollas et al., 2022). In this category, S1, S2, S3 and S4 present negative values, i.e., the products generated promote environmen- tal benefits capable of mitigating the impact generated. In general, the avoided use of nitrogen and phosphorus, and the use of heat and conse- quent avoided use of wood, were the most significant steps that justify these favorable values in the scenarios. The use of the resources available in the waste, especially in terms of substitutes for commercial fertilizers, presents a benefit in all categories, the reduction of impacts related to the production and transport of synthetic fertilizers, reflects directly on the life cycle of these systems. Thus, the recovery of these resources in a context of depletion of natural sources, such as P, is essential for the promotion of food security combined with the reduction of environmental damage (Walling and Vaneeckhaute, 2020).

The FE and TE categories are the only ones in which the SB is configured as having one of the lowest impacts in relation to the other scenarios. This occurs in terms of FE, due to the emission of the P module in relation to the discharge of effluent into water bodies, which still presents a load of P and

N contributing to water contamination in this aspect, in addition to the de- mand for chemical additives to promote the precipitation of P. As in S4 the effluent is reused for swine rearing facilities, there is no such discharge and therefore the results of this configuration are the best. The possibility of having water for reuse at the end of the process is extremely important in a context of water scarcity. Ensuring this resource, especially considering the high water consumption that pig farming presents, is essential for the viability of this activity (Ijoma et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2020). Thus, as the other scenarios also have the capacity to recover the resource, their damage can be reduced if this practice is implemented. Besides the benefit of water reuse, the use of solid fertilizers promotes a better use of the resources with less losses to the environment, due to the slow release of nutrients, unlike liquid biofertilizers that end up having greater losses by leaching, contribut- ing to improve the performance of this scenario (Corbala-Robles et al., 2018; Jeong et al., 2018).

For TE, in S4 the benefits promoted by resource recovery are not able to reduce the damages, due to the losses in the process, the composting is re- sponsible for about 70 % of the impacts, making this system present the worst performance. In the other systems the impacts are consistent with the findings of Ugwu et al. (2022) and Ramírez-Arpide et al. (2018) where the emission of CHP was one of the critical steps in this category. However, it is possible to verify negative values for TE in the other scenarios (SB, S1, S2 and S3), that is, they present the complete mitigation of the damages generated, this due to the recoveries of NPK fertilizers, which mit- igate on average 11 + 12% of the damages. In addition, heat and power utilization contribute on average for 20 + 20.5 of the benefits of the systems in scenarios S1, S2, S3 and S4.

Due to the large influence of the fertilization step, a sensitivity analysis was performed to verify the effects of different emission factors on the performance of the impact categories evaluated. In general, the highest sen- sitivities were seen for the categories Global warming, Stratospheric ozone depletion and Terrestrial acidification, for scenarios S1, S2 and S3 (Fig. 2.7a in supplementary material). While for S4, in addition to these the category Freshwater eutrophication was also one of the categories that showed the greatest variations.

For Global warming the 40 increase or decrease in emission rates from fertilizer application promoted changes of 25 + 2% on average in GHG emis- sions for S1, S2 and S3, and 37 + % for 60% change in emission rates from fertilizer application. While for S4 the change was around % (to + %) and % for increase or decrease in emission rates from fertilizer appli- cation. For Terrestrial acidification, scenarios S1 and S3 were the most sensi- tive with changes averaging 46 + 6% (to + 40%). In the Stratospheric ozone depletion category, scenarios S2 and S3 presented a change around considering an increase or decrease of 60 in fertilization emission rates.

In S4 Freshwater eutrophication ranged from 13 to 0%, with increased or decreased emissions of 40 and 60%. These results corroborate those ob- served by Duan et al. (2020), who verified in sensitivity studies of emissions from the application of digestate, that emissions from fertilization should not be underestimated, and therefore the adoption of good practices is fun- damental to make these technological routes viable.

The improvement of the processes highlights the importance of waste management for the minimization of environmental damage. The choice of technologies and treatment routes has a direct impact on the damage to the environment, as can be seen in the present results. Furthermore, the advantages of using energy from the swine waste are evident with the reduction of impacts in all categories compared to the scenario without using energy from the waste, AD plays a key role in this sense, since it cap- tures the noxious gases and enables energy recovery from them (Fermoso et al., 2018). Thus, corroborating the technical data, the routes that priori- tized the AD, present in S1 and S3, presented the best environmental perfor- mances (Yong et al., 2021).
62: Table 4 presents the environmental performance of the scenarios under study as well as an additional scenario (SB) to better understand the signif- icance of adopting treatment technologies. Analyzing the results by impact category, for GW of the steps involved in SB, it is possible to verify that the step that contributes most significantly to the GW potential are the fertiliza- tion processes, emitting 147 kg of CO2 per m³ of waste and representing 60 % of the total value of this category, in addition to the step of storing the waste for 80 days, which presents 35 % of the impacts of this category for this scenario, as illustrated in Fig. 4. Kuhn et al. (2018) discuss that CH4 emissions from storage and direct N2O emissions after manure applica- tion are the main causes of GW potential, like the results observed in the present study.

CH4 losses in the Bio module are the main cause of damage in this category, according to Kvist and Aryal (2019)

The monitoring and identification of these leaks are fundamental for the optimization of the process, investment in technological systems can mini- mize the effect of losses. In N module, N2O losses are the main cause of im- pact in the GW category, not different from what was observed by Riaño and García-González (2015), who discussed that the treatment of swine waste via initrification/denitrification was responsible for total annual
Fig. 4. Environmental impacts of each phase of the scenarios under study for the life cycle of one m³ of swine manure.
emissions of 35 tons CO2 eq, also attributed to direct emissions of N2O in the biological reactor.

In the other scenarios S2, S3 and S4 the same behavior for the GW cat- egory is observed, i.e. the fertilization step and the N module emissions are the most significant steps for the category. Despite these losses, adoption of treatment systems (S1, S2, S3 and S4) promoted a mitigation of 86 + 3% of GW potential compared to the baseline scenario SB. Also, for scenario S2 the emissions from the storage pond (7.96 CO2 also contribute signif- icantly to the final impact and in S4 the composting step contributes of the impacts in the category. Jeong et al. (2018) observed that composting of manure increased the GW potential by 35 compared to the untreated scenarios, however the use of compost for fertilizing rice paddies had a ben- eficial impact on reducing CH4 losses, due to the generation of a stable com- post with lower volume but high fertilizing power. In the present study, the energy matrix is renewable based on hydraulic sources, which ends up the most significant impacts tending towards the stage that makes use of fossil sources, in this case the use of fuels for soil application (Goulart Coelho and Lange, 2018; Li et al., 2021).

The ammonia losses by volatilization in the composting stage of S4, make it present the worst result regarding TA, about 88 % higher than SB. In the other scenarios this emission is more significant in the N module, for S2 (8.24E-01 SO2 eq) and S3 (2.64E-01 SO2 due to the higher pH, which contributes to NH3-N losses; besides the application to the soil, which corresponds to 2.08E-01 kg SO2 eq in the S1, 2.95E-01 kg SO2 in the S2, 2.99E-01 kg SO2 eq in the S3 and 1.34 kg SO2 eq in the SB. In the SOD category, the SB also presents an impact on average 85 + 4% higher than the other scenarios evaluated, with of the impact attributed to the fertilization step, the most significant step also for the other scenarios, contributing on average 65 + of the impact. In this category the losses of N2O and NO caused by fertilization are the main contributors to the adverse burden, in addition, the losses in the composting stage of scenario S4 are responsible for the emission of 6.85E-04 kg CFC11 eq

The fertilization stage is one of the largest contributors to the environ- mental burden of GW potential in all scenarios, as well as to the categories of SOD and TA, due to the losses promoted both by the sprinkling applica- tion process, which promotes the loss of nutrients and gases, and the use of fossil fuels, which promote the release of heavy metals, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and other toxic compounds into the environment (Ioannou- Ttofa et al., 2021). Duan et al. (2020) found a positive correlation between the distance from the farms and the biogas plants, the reducing the trans- port route by 5 km promoted a reduction of 118° of damage in GW poten- tial, while an increase in the distance traveled to 100 km increases damage by 629 %, which corroborates the data of the present study, since the volume of sludge and consequent increase in the area of application was the main divergence between the scenarios.

The impacts of the OFTE category are mainly due to emissions from the CHP plant (for S1, S2, S3 and S4) and the composting module in the S4, while in SB fertilization is the most significant damage step (98 %), with the NOx and methane losses involved in these steps being the main environ- mental burdens (Hollas et al., 2022). In this category, S1, S2, S3 and S4 present negative values, i.e., the products generated promote environmen- tal benefits capable of mitigating the impact generated. In general, the avoided use of nitrogen and phosphorus, and the use of heat and conse- quent avoided use of wood, were the most significant steps that justify these favorable values in the scenarios. The use of the resources available in the waste, especially in terms of substitutes for commercial fertilizers, presents a benefit in all categories, the reduction of impacts related to the production and transport of synthetic fertilizers, reflects directly on the life cycle of these systems. Thus, the recovery of these resources in a context of depletion of natural sources, such as P, is essential for the promotion of food security combined with the reduction of environmental damage (Walling and Vaneeckhaute, 2020).

The FE and TE categories are the only ones in which the SB is configured as having one of the lowest impacts in relation to the other scenarios. This occurs in terms of FE, due to the emission of the P module in relation to the discharge of effluent into water bodies, which still presents a load of P and

N contributing to water contamination in this aspect, in addition to the de- mand for chemical additives to promote the precipitation of P. As in S4 the effluent is reused for swine rearing facilities, there is no such discharge and therefore the results of this configuration are the best. The possibility of having water for reuse at the end of the process is extremely important in a context of water scarcity. Ensuring this resource, especially considering the high water consumption that pig farming presents, is essential for the viability of this activity (Ijoma et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2020). Thus, as the other scenarios also have the capacity to recover the resource, their damage can be reduced if this practice is implemented. Besides the benefit of water reuse, the use of solid fertilizers promotes a better use of the resources with less losses to the environment, due to the slow release of nutrients, unlike liquid biofertilizers that end up having greater losses by leaching, contribut- ing to improve the performance of this scenario (Corbala-Robles et al., 2018; Jeong et al., 2018).

For TE, in S4 the benefits promoted by resource recovery are not able to reduce the damages, due to the losses in the process, the composting is re- sponsible for about 70 % of the impacts, making this system present the worst performance. In the other systems the impacts are consistent with the findings of Ugwu et al. (2022) and Ramírez-Arpide et al. (2018) where the emission of CHP was one of the critical steps in this category. However, it is possible to verify negative values for TE in the other scenarios (SB, S1, S2 and S3), that is, they present the complete mitigation of the damages generated, this due to the recoveries of NPK fertilizers, which mit- igate on average 11 + 12% of the damages. In addition, heat and power utilization contribute on average for 20 + 20.5 of the benefits of the systems in scenarios S1, S2, S3 and S4.

Due to the large influence of the fertilization step, a sensitivity analysis was performed to verify the effects of different emission factors on the performance of the impact categories evaluated. In general, the highest sen- sitivities were seen for the categories Global warming, Stratospheric ozone depletion and Terrestrial acidification, for scenarios S1, S2 and S3 (Fig. 2.7a in supplementary material). While for S4, in addition to these the category Freshwater eutrophication was also one of the categories that showed the greatest variations.

For Global warming the 40 increase or decrease in emission rates from fertilizer application promoted changes of 25 + 2% on average in GHG emis- sions for S1, S2 and S3, and 37 + % for 60% change in emission rates from fertilizer application. While for S4 the change was around % (to + %) and % for increase or decrease in emission rates from fertilizer appli- cation. For Terrestrial acidification, scenarios S1 and S3 were the most sensi- tive with changes averaging 46 + 6% (to + 40%). In the Stratospheric ozone depletion category, scenarios S2 and S3 presented a change around considering an increase or decrease of 60 in fertilization emission rates.

In S4 Freshwater eutrophication ranged from 13 to 0%, with increased or decreased emissions of 40 and 60%. These results corroborate those ob- served by Duan et al. (2020), who verified in sensitivity studies of emissions from the application of digestate, that emissions from fertilization should not be underestimated, and therefore the adoption of good practices is fun- damental to make these technological routes viable.

The improvement of the processes highlights the importance of waste management for the minimization of environmental damage. The choice of technologies and treatment routes has a direct impact on the damage to the environment, as can be seen in the present results. Furthermore, the advantages of using energy from the swine waste are evident with the reduction of impacts in all categories compared to the scenario without using energy from the waste, AD plays a key role in this sense, since it cap- tures the noxious gases and enables energy recovery from them (Fermoso et al., 2018). Thus, corroborating the technical data, the routes that priori- tized the AD, present in S1 and S3, presented the best environmental perfor- mances (Yong et al., 2021).
65: Table 4 presents the environmental performance of the scenarios under study as well as an additional scenario (SB) to better understand the signif- icance of adopting treatment technologies. Analyzing the results by impact category, for GW of the steps involved in SB, it is possible to verify that the step that contributes most significantly to the GW potential are the fertiliza- tion processes, emitting 147 kg of CO2 per m³ of waste and representing 60 % of the total value of this category, in addition to the step of storing the waste for 80 days, which presents 35 % of the impacts of this category for this scenario, as illustrated in Fig. 4. Kuhn et al. (2018) discuss that CH4 emissions from storage and direct N2O emissions after manure applica- tion are the main causes of GW potential, like the results observed in the present study.

Analyzing the results by impact category, for GW of the steps involved in SB, it is possible to verify that the step that contributes most significantly to the GW potential are the fertiliza- tion processes, emitting 147 kg of CO2 per m³ of waste and representing 60 % of the total value of this category, in addition to the step of storing the waste for 80 days, which presents 35 % of the impacts of this category for this scenario, as illustrated in Fig. 4. Kuhn et al. (2018) discuss that CH4 emissions from storage and direct N2O emissions after manure applica- tion are the main causes of GW potential, like the results observed in the present study.
Table 4 Results of the environmental impact assessment for all scenarios.

|SB|S1|S2|S3|S4 Global warming (kg CO2 eq)|221|21.6|31.6|27.7|38.9 Stratospheric ozone depletion (kg CFC11 eq)|5.29E-03|5.65E-04|7.60E-04|7.65E-04|1.05E-03 Ozone formation, Terrestrial ecosystems (kg NO eq)|4.77E-01|2.40E-02|-1.82E-02|-2.35E-02|1.45E-02 Terrestrial acidification (kg SO2 eq)|1.32|2.04E-01|1.13|5.91E-01|2.48 Freshwater eutrophication (kg P eq)|1.99E-03|1.00E-02|3.85E-03|7.21E-03|1.37E-03 Terrestrial ecotoxicity (kg 1,4-DCB)|-15.8|-14.3|-10.9|-15.8|26.4

In S1, besides the fertilization stage (39%) and the emissions from the storage pond (3.33 kg CO2 eq), the Bio module (9 %) and the N module (12 %) are potential sources of atmospheric emissions, either by leakage losses in the case of the Bio module or by direct emission from processes in the case of the N module. CH4 losses in the Bio module are the main cause of damage in this category, according to Kvist and Aryal (2019)

The monitoring and identification of these leaks are fundamental for the optimization of the process, investment in technological systems can mini- mize the effect of losses. In N module, N2O losses are the main cause of im- pact in the GW category, not different from what was observed by Riaño and García-González (2015), who discussed that the treatment of swine waste via initrification/denitrification was responsible for total annual
Fig. 4. Environmental impacts of each phase of the scenarios under study for the life cycle of one m³ of swine manure.
emissions of 35 tons CO2 eq, also attributed to direct emissions of N2O in the biological reactor.

In the other scenarios S2, S3 and S4 the same behavior for the GW cat- egory is observed, i.e. the fertilization step and the N module emissions are the most significant steps for the category. Despite these losses, adoption of treatment systems (S1, S2, S3 and S4) promoted a mitigation of 86 + 3% of GW potential compared to the baseline scenario SB. Also, for scenario S2 the emissions from the storage pond (7.96 CO2 also contribute signif- icantly to the final impact and in S4 the composting step contributes of the impacts in the category. Jeong et al. (2018) observed that composting of manure increased the GW potential by 35 compared to the untreated scenarios, however the use of compost for fertilizing rice paddies had a ben- eficial impact on reducing CH4 losses, due to the generation of a stable com- post with lower volume but high fertilizing power. In the present study, the energy matrix is renewable based on hydraulic sources, which ends up the most significant impacts tending towards the stage that makes use of fossil sources, in this case the use of fuels for soil application (Goulart Coelho and Lange, 2018; Li et al., 2021).

The ammonia losses by volatilization in the composting stage of S4, make it present the worst result regarding TA, about 88 % higher than SB. In the other scenarios this emission is more significant in the N module, for S2 (8.24E-01 SO2 eq) and S3 (2.64E-01 SO2 due to the higher pH, which contributes to NH3-N losses; besides the application to the soil, which corresponds to 2.08E-01 kg SO2 eq in the S1, 2.95E-01 kg SO2 in the S2, 2.99E-01 kg SO2 eq in the S3 and 1.34 kg SO2 eq in the SB. In the SOD category, the SB also presents an impact on average 85 + 4% higher than the other scenarios evaluated, with of the impact attributed to the fertilization step, the most significant step also for the other scenarios, contributing on average 65 + of the impact. In this category the losses of N2O and NO caused by fertilization are the main contributors to the adverse burden, in addition, the losses in the composting stage of scenario S4 are responsible for the emission of 6.85E-04 kg CFC11 eq

The fertilization stage is one of the largest contributors to the environ- mental burden of GW potential in all scenarios, as well as to the categories of SOD and TA, due to the losses promoted both by the sprinkling applica- tion process, which promotes the loss of nutrients and gases, and the use of fossil fuels, which promote the release of heavy metals, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and other toxic compounds into the environment (Ioannou- Ttofa et al., 2021). Duan et al. (2020) found a positive correlation between the distance from the farms and the biogas plants, the reducing the trans- port route by 5 km promoted a reduction of 118° of damage in GW poten- tial, while an increase in the distance traveled to 100 km increases damage by 629 %, which corroborates the data of the present study, since the volume of sludge and consequent increase in the area of application was the main divergence between the scenarios.

The impacts of the OFTE category are mainly due to emissions from the CHP plant (for S1, S2, S3 and S4) and the composting module in the S4, while in SB fertilization is the most significant damage step (98 %), with the NOx and methane losses involved in these steps being the main environ- mental burdens (Hollas et al., 2022). In this category, S1, S2, S3 and S4 present negative values, i.e., the products generated promote environmen- tal benefits capable of mitigating the impact generated. In general, the avoided use of nitrogen and phosphorus, and the use of heat and conse- quent avoided use of wood, were the most significant steps that justify these favorable values in the scenarios. The use of the resources available in the waste, especially in terms of substitutes for commercial fertilizers, presents a benefit in all categories, the reduction of impacts related to the production and transport of synthetic fertilizers, reflects directly on the life cycle of these systems. Thus, the recovery of these resources in a context of depletion of natural sources, such as P, is essential for the promotion of food security combined with the reduction of environmental damage (Walling and Vaneeckhaute, 2020).

The FE and TE categories are the only ones in which the SB is configured as having one of the lowest impacts in relation to the other scenarios. This occurs in terms of FE, due to the emission of the P module in relation to the discharge of effluent into water bodies, which still presents a load of P and

N contributing to water contamination in this aspect, in addition to the de- mand for chemical additives to promote the precipitation of P. As in S4 the effluent is reused for swine rearing facilities, there is no such discharge and therefore the results of this configuration are the best. The possibility of having water for reuse at the end of the process is extremely important in a context of water scarcity. Ensuring this resource, especially considering the high water consumption that pig farming presents, is essential for the viability of this activity (Ijoma et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2020). Thus, as the other scenarios also have the capacity to recover the resource, their damage can be reduced if this practice is implemented. Besides the benefit of water reuse, the use of solid fertilizers promotes a better use of the resources with less losses to the environment, due to the slow release of nutrients, unlike liquid biofertilizers that end up having greater losses by leaching, contribut- ing to improve the performance of this scenario (Corbala-Robles et al., 2018; Jeong et al., 2018).

For TE, in S4 the benefits promoted by resource recovery are not able to reduce the damages, due to the losses in the process, the composting is re- sponsible for about 70 % of the impacts, making this system present the worst performance. In the other systems the impacts are consistent with the findings of Ugwu et al. (2022) and Ramírez-Arpide et al. (2018) where the emission of CHP was one of the critical steps in this category. However, it is possible to verify negative values for TE in the other scenarios (SB, S1, S2 and S3), that is, they present the complete mitigation of the damages generated, this due to the recoveries of NPK fertilizers, which mit- igate on average 11 + 12% of the damages. In addition, heat and power utilization contribute on average for 20 + 20.5 of the benefits of the systems in scenarios S1, S2, S3 and S4.

Due to the large influence of the fertilization step, a sensitivity analysis was performed to verify the effects of different emission factors on the performance of the impact categories evaluated. In general, the highest sen- sitivities were seen for the categories Global warming, Stratospheric ozone depletion and Terrestrial acidification, for scenarios S1, S2 and S3 (Fig. 2.7a in supplementary material). While for S4, in addition to these the category Freshwater eutrophication was also one of the categories that showed the greatest variations.

For Global warming the 40 increase or decrease in emission rates from fertilizer application promoted changes of 25 + 2% on average in GHG emis- sions for S1, S2 and S3, and 37 + % for 60% change in emission rates from fertilizer application. While for S4 the change was around % (to + %) and % for increase or decrease in emission rates from fertilizer appli- cation. For Terrestrial acidification, scenarios S1 and S3 were the most sensi- tive with changes averaging 46 + 6% (to + 40%). In the Stratospheric ozone depletion category, scenarios S2 and S3 presented a change around considering an increase or decrease of 60 in fertilization emission rates.

In S4 Freshwater eutrophication ranged from 13 to 0%, with increased or decreased emissions of 40 and 60%. These results corroborate those ob- served by Duan et al. (2020), who verified in sensitivity studies of emissions from the application of digestate, that emissions from fertilization should not be underestimated, and therefore the adoption of good practices is fun- damental to make these technological routes viable.

The improvement of the processes highlights the importance of waste management for the minimization of environmental damage. The choice of technologies and treatment routes has a direct impact on the damage to the environment, as can be seen in the present results. Furthermore, the advantages of using energy from the swine waste are evident with the reduction of impacts in all categories compared to the scenario without using energy from the waste, AD plays a key role in this sense, since it cap- tures the noxious gases and enables energy recovery from them (Fermoso et al., 2018). Thus, corroborating the technical data, the routes that priori- tized the AD, present in S1 and S3, presented the best environmental perfor- mances (Yong et al., 2021).

The improvement of the processes highlights the importance of waste management for the minimization of environmental damage. The choice of technologies and treatment routes has a direct impact on the damage to the environment, as can be seen in the present results. Furthermore, the advantages of using energy from the swine waste are evident with the reduction of impacts in all categories compared to the scenario without using energy from the waste, AD plays a key role in this sense, since it cap- tures the noxious gases and enables energy recovery from them (Fermoso et al., 2018). Thus, corroborating the technical data, the routes that priori- tized the AD, present in S1 and S3, presented the best environmental perfor- mances (Yong et al., 2021).
70: Table 4 presents the environmental performance of the scenarios under study as well as an additional scenario (SB) to better understand the signif- icance of adopting treatment technologies. Analyzing the results by impact category, for GW of the steps involved in SB, it is possible to verify that the step that contributes most significantly to the GW potential are the fertiliza- tion processes, emitting 147 kg of CO2 per m³ of waste and representing 60 % of the total value of this category, in addition to the step of storing the waste for 80 days, which presents 35 % of the impacts of this category for this scenario, as illustrated in Fig. 4. Kuhn et al. (2018) discuss that CH4 emissions from storage and direct N2O emissions after manure applica- tion are the main causes of GW potential, like the results observed in the present study.

In S1, besides the fertilization stage (39%) and the emissions from the storage pond (3.33 kg CO2 eq), the Bio module (9 %) and the N module (12 %) are potential sources of atmospheric emissions, either by leakage losses in the case of the Bio module or by direct emission from processes in the case of the N module. CH4 losses in the Bio module are the main cause of damage in this category, according to Kvist and Aryal (2019)

The monitoring and identification of these leaks are fundamental for the optimization of the process, investment in technological systems can mini- mize the effect of losses. In N module, N2O losses are the main cause of im- pact in the GW category, not different from what was observed by Riaño and García-González (2015), who discussed that the treatment of swine waste via initrification/denitrification was responsible for total annual
Fig. 4. Environmental impacts of each phase of the scenarios under study for the life cycle of one m³ of swine manure.
emissions of 35 tons CO2 eq, also attributed to direct emissions of N2O in the biological reactor.

In the other scenarios S2, S3 and S4 the same behavior for the GW cat- egory is observed, i.e. the fertilization step and the N module emissions are the most significant steps for the category. Despite these losses, adoption of treatment systems (S1, S2, S3 and S4) promoted a mitigation of 86 + 3% of GW potential compared to the baseline scenario SB. Also, for scenario S2 the emissions from the storage pond (7.96 CO2 also contribute signif- icantly to the final impact and in S4 the composting step contributes of the impacts in the category. Jeong et al. (2018) observed that composting of manure increased the GW potential by 35 compared to the untreated scenarios, however the use of compost for fertilizing rice paddies had a ben- eficial impact on reducing CH4 losses, due to the generation of a stable com- post with lower volume but high fertilizing power. In the present study, the energy matrix is renewable based on hydraulic sources, which ends up the most significant impacts tending towards the stage that makes use of fossil sources, in this case the use of fuels for soil application (Goulart Coelho and Lange, 2018; Li et al., 2021).

The ammonia losses by volatilization in the composting stage of S4, make it present the worst result regarding TA, about 88 % higher than SB. In the other scenarios this emission is more significant in the N module, for S2 (8.24E-01 SO2 eq) and S3 (2.64E-01 SO2 due to the higher pH, which contributes to NH3-N losses; besides the application to the soil, which corresponds to 2.08E-01 kg SO2 eq in the S1, 2.95E-01 kg SO2 in the S2, 2.99E-01 kg SO2 eq in the S3 and 1.34 kg SO2 eq in the SB. In the SOD category, the SB also presents an impact on average 85 + 4% higher than the other scenarios evaluated, with of the impact attributed to the fertilization step, the most significant step also for the other scenarios, contributing on average 65 + of the impact. In this category the losses of N2O and NO caused by fertilization are the main contributors to the adverse burden, in addition, the losses in the composting stage of scenario S4 are responsible for the emission of 6.85E-04 kg CFC11 eq

The fertilization stage is one of the largest contributors to the environ- mental burden of GW potential in all scenarios, as well as to the categories of SOD and TA, due to the losses promoted both by the sprinkling applica- tion process, which promotes the loss of nutrients and gases, and the use of fossil fuels, which promote the release of heavy metals, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and other toxic compounds into the environment (Ioannou- Ttofa et al., 2021). Duan et al. (2020) found a positive correlation between the distance from the farms and the biogas plants, the reducing the trans- port route by 5 km promoted a reduction of 118° of damage in GW poten- tial, while an increase in the distance traveled to 100 km increases damage by 629 %, which corroborates the data of the present study, since the volume of sludge and consequent increase in the area of application was the main divergence between the scenarios.

The impacts of the OFTE category are mainly due to emissions from the CHP plant (for S1, S2, S3 and S4) and the composting module in the S4, while in SB fertilization is the most significant damage step (98 %), with the NOx and methane losses involved in these steps being the main environ- mental burdens (Hollas et al., 2022). In this category, S1, S2, S3 and S4 present negative values, i.e., the products generated promote environmen- tal benefits capable of mitigating the impact generated. In general, the avoided use of nitrogen and phosphorus, and the use of heat and conse- quent avoided use of wood, were the most significant steps that justify these favorable values in the scenarios. The use of the resources available in the waste, especially in terms of substitutes for commercial fertilizers, presents a benefit in all categories, the reduction of impacts related to the production and transport of synthetic fertilizers, reflects directly on the life cycle of these systems. Thus, the recovery of these resources in a context of depletion of natural sources, such as P, is essential for the promotion of food security combined with the reduction of environmental damage (Walling and Vaneeckhaute, 2020).

The FE and TE categories are the only ones in which the SB is configured as having one of the lowest impacts in relation to the other scenarios. This occurs in terms of FE, due to the emission of the P module in relation to the discharge of effluent into water bodies, which still presents a load of P and

N contributing to water contamination in this aspect, in addition to the de- mand for chemical additives to promote the precipitation of P. As in S4 the effluent is reused for swine rearing facilities, there is no such discharge and therefore the results of this configuration are the best. The possibility of having water for reuse at the end of the process is extremely important in a context of water scarcity. Ensuring this resource, especially considering the high water consumption that pig farming presents, is essential for the viability of this activity (Ijoma et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2020). Thus, as the other scenarios also have the capacity to recover the resource, their damage can be reduced if this practice is implemented. Besides the benefit of water reuse, the use of solid fertilizers promotes a better use of the resources with less losses to the environment, due to the slow release of nutrients, unlike liquid biofertilizers that end up having greater losses by leaching, contribut- ing to improve the performance of this scenario (Corbala-Robles et al., 2018; Jeong et al., 2018).

For TE, in S4 the benefits promoted by resource recovery are not able to reduce the damages, due to the losses in the process, the composting is re- sponsible for about 70 % of the impacts, making this system present the worst performance. In the other systems the impacts are consistent with the findings of Ugwu et al. (2022) and Ramírez-Arpide et al. (2018) where the emission of CHP was one of the critical steps in this category. However, it is possible to verify negative values for TE in the other scenarios (SB, S1, S2 and S3), that is, they present the complete mitigation of the damages generated, this due to the recoveries of NPK fertilizers, which mit- igate on average 11 + 12% of the damages. In addition, heat and power utilization contribute on average for 20 + 20.5 of the benefits of the systems in scenarios S1, S2, S3 and S4.

Due to the large influence of the fertilization step, a sensitivity analysis was performed to verify the effects of different emission factors on the performance of the impact categories evaluated. In general, the highest sen- sitivities were seen for the categories Global warming, Stratospheric ozone depletion and Terrestrial acidification, for scenarios S1, S2 and S3 (Fig. 2.7a in supplementary material). While for S4, in addition to these the category Freshwater eutrophication was also one of the categories that showed the greatest variations.

For Global warming the 40 increase or decrease in emission rates from fertilizer application promoted changes of 25 + 2% on average in GHG emis- sions for S1, S2 and S3, and 37 + % for 60% change in emission rates from fertilizer application. While for S4 the change was around % (to + %) and % for increase or decrease in emission rates from fertilizer appli- cation. For Terrestrial acidification, scenarios S1 and S3 were the most sensi- tive with changes averaging 46 + 6% (to + 40%). In the Stratospheric ozone depletion category, scenarios S2 and S3 presented a change around considering an increase or decrease of 60 in fertilization emission rates.

In S4 Freshwater eutrophication ranged from 13 to 0%, with increased or decreased emissions of 40 and 60%. These results corroborate those ob- served by Duan et al. (2020), who verified in sensitivity studies of emissions from the application of digestate, that emissions from fertilization should not be underestimated, and therefore the adoption of good practices is fun- damental to make these technological routes viable.

The improvement of the processes highlights the importance of waste management for the minimization of environmental damage. The choice of technologies and treatment routes has a direct impact on the damage to the environment, as can be seen in the present results. Furthermore, the advantages of using energy from the swine waste are evident with the reduction of impacts in all categories compared to the scenario without using energy from the waste, AD plays a key role in this sense, since it cap- tures the noxious gases and enables energy recovery from them (Fermoso et al., 2018). Thus, corroborating the technical data, the routes that priori- tized the AD, present in S1 and S3, presented the best environmental perfor- mances (Yong et al., 2021).
71: Although technically and environmentally the scenarios under study present relatively favorable values, economically it was possible to verify

greater distancing between the proposed technological routes. For S1 and S2 the NPV and IRR values were favorable, presenting profit in the simula- tions performed, with a cost-benefit ratio of 1.82 for S1 and 1.46 for S2 (Fig. 5). The previously discussed improvement of S1 in terms of energy utilization and process management, reflects directly on the costs and rev- enues of the systems.

Scenarios S3 and S4 required the largest capital investment, about 42 higher, and a 70 % increase in operating costs, due to the P module, the UASB reactors and the composting unit, and did not show a profit. In these scenarios, the total revenue for S3 was U$ 3.52 per m³, 3 and U$ 2.56 per m³ for S4, however, the net revenues (subtracting OPEX) per m³ are negative, thus generating a net cost in treatment of U$ 0.66 per m³ and U $ 3.21 per m³, 3 for S3 and S4 respectively. The use of sodium hydroxide instead of calcium hydroxide in S3 and S4 makes the process more expen- sive, besides this, for the S4 there is still the cost of the wood chips for composting. This behavior was also verified by De Vrieze et al. (2019), in the scenarios in which the authors considered P recovery through struvite, there was an increase in the gross cost of treatment from €13.74 to €19.23 per ton of manure, compared to the scenarios that did not feature this treat- ment unit.

For all scenarios the capital cost and depreciation (39 + %), in addi- tion to raw material (15 + 17%) and maintenance (23 + 4%) costs are the ones that govern treatment system charges (Fig. 4.1a in supplementary material). This profile is common to economic evaluations of waste AD systems. For systems operational costs, the highest values are for N module for S1 (44%) and S2 (47%) and P module, for S3 (34%) and S4 (45%). Aui et al. (2019) highlight that AD systems are expensive, accounting for most of the investments, these results were also observed in the present study, where about 31 + 6% of the investments are due to the facilities required for the AD modules (including the SLS, the AD reactors and the CHP unit).

Despite the favorable results for S1 and S2, it is worth noting that the economic benefits of using the nutrients from the sludge as substitutes for commercial fertilizers within the production unit were considered. If the production unit has no agricultural area for the use of these resources, only the generation of electricity will count as revenue, since the resulting sludge should be made available for withdrawal by neighbors with produc- tive area, without added value, in this conditions NPV of all scenarios is negative. Castro e Silva et al. (2022), also verified the dependence on the valuation of biofertilizers to promote the viability of the systems, according to the data obtained by the authors 72% of revenues were from the market- ing of biofertilizers, which directly reflected in the positive NPV of the scenarios.

Without the revenues from nutrient recovery the net costs of treatment increase per m³ to U$ 1.62 in S1, U$ 2.30 in S2, U$ 3.03 for S3 and U$ 5.06 in S4. The use of these resources is of extreme importance, and not only in

economic terms to favor the implementation of the system, but also on a global level as a valuable source of nutrients with agronomic potential able to supply the demand for commercial inputs (Awasthi et al., 2022). Although in Brazil the sale of biofertilizers is regulated by Normative Instruction SDA/MAPA No. 61/2020 (MAPA and SDA, 2020), the commer- cialization of this type of product is not yet a common practice. These resi- dues are not valued, mainly due to the water content, which makes the transportation and disposal of these sludges difficult. Because of this, addi- tional steps, such as drying, or adopting other recovery technologies, to pro- cess these sludges may be alternatives to transport the solid fraction in a more economical way, to more distant locations, with a market value, con- tributing economically to the scenarios (Pexas et al., 2021).

Besides the valorization of the sludge in the system, another point that can help in reducing the costs of the processes is related to the environmental gain promoted by the treatment, because the considerable reduction in emis- sions of atmospheric pollutants. Therefore, the marketing of carbon credits, or the valuation of meat produced in a low carbon emission system, may be an alternative to assist in the economic viability (Kunatsa and Xia, 2022). In Brazil, the Sectoral Plan for Adaptation and Low Carbon Emission in Agriculture and Livestock, named ABC+, has the goal of CO2 equivalent emissions reduction by 1.1 billion tons in the agricultural and livestock sector by 2030. With this, the public sector has been promoting discussions for the regulation of carbon credit markets, in order to value the environmental con- tributions promoted by productive systems that present neutrality or initia- tives for the mitigation of environmental impacts (Milhorance et al., 2022).

Furthermore, guidelines are already being studied for the certification of neutral beef production with the brands " Beef Neutral Carbon" and " Beef Low Carbon " referring to sustainable production systems for farms that adopt the practices defined in the guidelines (Almeida and Alves, 2020; Lucchese-Cheung et al., 2021). These models can be expanded to other livestock sectors, including swine farming, contributing to the valori- zation of the products, and consequently adding income to the producers. This is a very important point, given the high investments required for the adoption of these treatment systems.

To achieve the goal of neutral carbon, the carbon credit market should accelerate in the coming periods, and the solutions discussed here can con- tribute to this perspective. In this context it is of fundamental importance to recognize and encourage the adoption of treatment measures such as those proposed. Fernando-Foncillas and Varrone (2021) discuss that the promo- tion of a greener and circular economy depends on public subsidies, the waste valorization process must be a matter discussed and adjusted through policies, which can promote the reduction of market risks, encouraging the adoption of these technologies.
Based on the aspects discussed and analyzing of the economic perfor- mances of the scenarios, it is noticeable that the adoption of such technologies
Fig. 5. Description of the economic indicators, in terms of NPV, IRR, and Payback from the evaluation of the economic viability of the systems under study.

can be feasible in a context of a low carbon economy. In this sense, AD plays a key role in treatment systems. The addition of income, especially by the bioenergy recovered in the AD, allows diluting the costs of the rest of the system, which are important units for the management of these wastes, capable of promoting the sustainability of the treatment systems (Awasthi et al., 2022).

The use of sodium hydroxide instead of calcium hydroxide in S3 and S4 makes the process more expen- sive, besides this, for the S4 there is still the cost of the wood chips for composting. This behavior was also verified by De Vrieze et al. (2019), in the scenarios in which the authors considered P recovery through struvite, there was an increase in the gross cost of treatment from €13.74 to €19.23 per ton of manure, compared to the scenarios that did not feature this treat- ment unit.

For all scenarios the capital cost and depreciation (39 + %), in addi- tion to raw material (15 + 17%) and maintenance (23 + 4%) costs are the ones that govern treatment system charges (Fig. 4.1a in supplementary material). This profile is common to economic evaluations of waste AD systems. For systems operational costs, the highest values are for N module for S1 (44%) and S2 (47%) and P module, for S3 (34%) and S4 (45%). Aui et al. (2019) highlight that AD systems are expensive, accounting for most of the investments, these results were also observed in the present study, where about 31 + 6% of the investments are due to the facilities required for the AD modules (including the SLS, the AD reactors and the CHP unit).

Despite the favorable results for S1 and S2, it is worth noting that the economic benefits of using the nutrients from the sludge as substitutes for commercial fertilizers within the production unit were considered. If the production unit has no agricultural area for the use of these resources, only the generation of electricity will count as revenue, since the resulting sludge should be made available for withdrawal by neighbors with produc- tive area, without added value, in this conditions NPV of all scenarios is negative. Castro e Silva et al. (2022), also verified the dependence on the valuation of biofertilizers to promote the viability of the systems, according to the data obtained by the authors 72% of revenues were from the market- ing of biofertilizers, which directly reflected in the positive NPV of the scenarios.

Without the revenues from nutrient recovery the net costs of treatment increase per m³ to U$ 1.62 in S1, U$ 2.30 in S2, U$ 3.03 for S3 and U$ 5.06 in S4. The use of these resources is of extreme importance, and not only in

economic terms to favor the implementation of the system, but also on a global level as a valuable source of nutrients with agronomic potential able to supply the demand for commercial inputs (Awasthi et al., 2022). Although in Brazil the sale of biofertilizers is regulated by Normative Instruction SDA/MAPA No. 61/2020 (MAPA and SDA, 2020), the commer- cialization of this type of product is not yet a common practice. These resi- dues are not valued, mainly due to the water content, which makes the transportation and disposal of these sludges difficult. Because of this, addi- tional steps, such as drying, or adopting other recovery technologies, to pro- cess these sludges may be alternatives to transport the solid fraction in a more economical way, to more distant locations, with a market value, con- tributing economically to the scenarios (Pexas et al., 2021).

Besides the valorization of the sludge in the system, another point that can help in reducing the costs of the processes is related to the environmental gain promoted by the treatment, because the considerable reduction in emis- sions of atmospheric pollutants. Therefore, the marketing of carbon credits, or the valuation of meat produced in a low carbon emission system, may be an alternative to assist in the economic viability (Kunatsa and Xia, 2022). In Brazil, the Sectoral Plan for Adaptation and Low Carbon Emission in Agriculture and Livestock, named ABC+, has the goal of CO2 equivalent emissions reduction by 1.1 billion tons in the agricultural and livestock sector by 2030. With this, the public sector has been promoting discussions for the regulation of carbon credit markets, in order to value the environmental con- tributions promoted by productive systems that present neutrality or initia- tives for the mitigation of environmental impacts (Milhorance et al., 2022).

Furthermore, guidelines are already being studied for the certification of neutral beef production with the brands " Beef Neutral Carbon" and " Beef Low Carbon " referring to sustainable production systems for farms that adopt the practices defined in the guidelines (Almeida and Alves, 2020; Lucchese-Cheung et al., 2021). These models can be expanded to other livestock sectors, including swine farming, contributing to the valori- zation of the products, and consequently adding income to the producers. This is a very important point, given the high investments required for the adoption of these treatment systems.

To achieve the goal of neutral carbon, the carbon credit market should accelerate in the coming periods, and the solutions discussed here can con- tribute to this perspective. In this context it is of fundamental importance to recognize and encourage the adoption of treatment measures such as those proposed. Fernando-Foncillas and Varrone (2021) discuss that the promo- tion of a greener and circular economy depends on public subsidies, the waste valorization process must be a matter discussed and adjusted through policies, which can promote the reduction of market risks, encouraging the adoption of these technologies.
Based on the aspects discussed and analyzing of the economic perfor- mances of the scenarios, it is noticeable that the adoption of such technologies
Fig. 5. Description of the economic indicators, in terms of NPV, IRR, and Payback from the evaluation of the economic viability of the systems under study.

can be feasible in a context of a low carbon economy. In this sense, AD plays a key role in treatment systems. The addition of income, especially by the bioenergy recovered in the AD, allows diluting the costs of the rest of the system, which are important units for the management of these wastes, capable of promoting the sustainability of the treatment systems (Awasthi et al., 2022).

79: <header>3.4. Practical implications in the circular economy context and future perspectives </header>

AD stands out in the context of promoting the circularity of treatment systems, not only by recovering energy from waste, and creating an alterna- tive renewable and sustainable source of energy, but also by generating so- cial and economic benefits with the creation of job opportunities and a market focused on this value chain (Awasthi et al., 2021, 2022). In this sense WBA (2021) discuss that AD is able to assist in promoting at least 9 of the 17 sustainable development goals established by the United Nations, i.e., assist the achievement of goal 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, 13 and 15; due to their direct or indirect participation and influence on these aspects, in social, en- vironmental, and economic terms.

Ijoma et al. (2022) highlight that biogas production should not be seen only as an alternative source of renewable energy, but as an opportunity to achieve carbon neutrality, without promoting losses in the productive sec- tors. This becomes evident when evaluating the results of the LCA of the treatment systems, moreover, the recovery of nutrients is fundamental to close the cycles of the agricultural sectors, this aspect still needs further discussion and technology to generate a commercial compost, given the influence that this revenue has on the economy of the treatment systems (Finzi et al., 2020).

Garrett et al. (2022) brings that the payment for environmental services, can also be an allied strategy for the adoption of systems focused on envi- ronmental preservation. Furthermore, the carbon credit market, is essential to promote sustainable waste management, making the adoption of these technologies attractive to the entire chain (Milhorance et al., 2022). Kapoor et al. (2020) highlights that the lack of more effective and targeted public policies creates uncertainty about these processes, delaying the tran- sition to a circular economy. Thus, the promotion of research focused on waste valorization, on the promotion of a more sustainable, low-carbon economy that encompasses the entire production chain, especially small producers, should be prioritized and conducted to assist the government in these directions for the sector. More LCA, technical and economic studies of the waste management models should be carried out to increase the information on the chains. Studies like this, or with a focus on the topics mentioned, help the sector to develop, promoting decarbonization, allied to the maintenance of the activities, obtaining clean and renewable energy. Despite the limitations of this study, mainly in terms of uncertainties and the sensitivity of the systems, the proposed treatment models presented optimistic results, demonstrating the ability to be applied and the benefits promoted by these measures. Sensitivity analyses of these models are im- portant to verify the behavior against fluctuations, SO future work should be conducted to explore these points to help consolidate the results and identify possible outliers.

82: Evaluation of the technical, environmental, and economic spheres of different configurations showed the importance of anaerobic digestion to make feasible the treatment routes of swine waste. A compact treatment process (SISTRATES was configured as a promising technology in circu- lar terms, presenting the best techno-economic, and environmental perfor- mances, with the final effluents of the treatment system able to be used, either as fertilizers, energy or as reuse water. The treatment technologies described in this study, guide the waste management with value addition and environmental damage minimization, contributing to decarbonization of the swine production chain, having anaerobic digestion as the promoter core.
